subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description: THIS RESEARCH DEVELOPS NEW ALGORITHMS AND THEIR THEORETICAL ANALYSES FOR THE DESIGN AND DECISION MAKING UNDER UNCERTAINTY AND INVOLVING COMPLEX SIMULATIONS. THE RICE UNIVERSITY TEAM DIRECTED BY PROF. HEINKENSCHLOSS WILL LEAD THE WORK ON TASK 10 ADAP TIVE METHODS FOR RISK AVERSE DESIGN AND CONTROL PROBLEMS, CO-LEAD THE WORK ON TASK 11 MULTI-STAGE FORMULATIONS OF OPTIMAL DESIGN UNDER UNCERTAINTY, AND INTERACT WITH OTHER PROJECT TEAMS ON OTHER TASKS. DURING THE THREE YEAR PROJECT PERIOD THE SUBT ASKS AND CORRESPONDING MILESTONES ARE: SUB-TASK 10.1. PROTOTYPE OPTIMIZATION ALGORITHMS AND SAMPLING METHODS FOR DIFFERENT FORMULATIONS OF RISK ADVERSE OPTIMIZATION PROBLEMS.  MILESTONE 10.1 (12 MONTHS):  DEMONSTRATE RELATIVE ADVANTAGES OF OPTIMIZATI ON FORMULATIONS AND SAMPLING STRATEGIES FOR DIFFERENT RISK MEASURES ON PROBLEMS RELATED TO TEST CASE, SUCH AS PROBLEMS GOVERNED BY SEMILINEAR CONVECTION DIFFUSION REACTION SYSTEMS. SUB-TASK 10.2  DEVELOP OPTIMIZATION FRAMEWORK AND CONVERGENCE THEORY TO INCLUDE ADAPTIVE SAMPLING STRATEGIES.  MILESTONES 10.2 (18 MONTHS): DEMONSTRATE ORDER OF MAGNITUDE PERFORMANCE INCREASE DUE TO ADAPTIVE SAMPLING IN EXAMPLES CONSIDERED IN SUB-TASK 10.2.  DEMONSTRATE APPLICABILITY TO DESIGN/CONTROL FORMULATIONS IN JET FLAME TEST PROBLEM. SUB-TASK 11.1  FORMULATE OPTIMAL DESIGN AND CONTROL AS MULTISTAGE DECISION PROBLEMS.  MILESTONE 11.1 (18MONTHS): FORMLATE THE MULTISTAGE DESIGN PROBLEM; ILLUSTRATE HOW MODELING APPROACH LEADS TO DIFFERENT DECISIONS COMPARED T O A CONVENTIONAL SINGLE-STAGE APPROACH FOR TARGET CONVECTION-DIFFUSION-REACTION MODEL PROBLEMS. SUB-TASK 11.2  EXPLORE TECHNIQUES TO IMPROVE EFFICIENCY FOR MULTISTAGE DESIGN, SUCH AS REDUCED MODELS, DECOMPOSITION AND ITERATIVE METHODS.  MILESTONE 11. 2 (27 MONTHS):  DEVISE DECOMPOSITION METHODOLOGIES FOR MULTISTAGE DESIGN PROBLEMS WITH DECISION-DEPENDENT UNCERTAINTIES; TEST ON TARGET CONVECTION-DIFFUSION-REACTION MODEL PROBLES; QUANTIFY THE SCALABILITY OF THE APPROACH WITH RESPECT TO THE DIMENSIO N OF THE DECISION SPACE COMPARED TO EXISTING APPROACHES THAT DO NOT EMPLOY DECOMPOSITION. SUB-TASK 11.3  RISK AND ROBUSTNESS IN OPTIMIZATION. prime_award_base_transaction_description: FuSe-TG: Co-Design of Chiral Quantum Photonic Devices and Circuits Integrated with 2D Material Heterostructures -Non-technical description: To deploy photons for real-world quantum technologies, it is essential to develop integrated photonic circuits consisting of various quantum photonic devices based on modern semiconductor materials with desired light emission, modulation, and detection properties. In this teaming project, a multidisciplinary team of researchers with combined expertise in materials, devices, and circuits explores a holistic, co-design approach toward this goal. The team investigates the quantum defects in two-dimensional materials for the emission of circularly polarized single photons. Further, the team explores strategies of synthesizing and simulating quantum photonic circuits while considering physical device limitations. The team conducts preliminary co-design research, initiates multidisciplinary curriculum development, and organizes three workshops by inviting researchers from academia and industry, particularly those from underrepresented groups, and students and educators, including those from local community colleges. Hence, the team can build capacity and develop communities and partnerships for future large research projects and semiconductor workforce development activities. Technical description: This teaming project consists of two co-design research explorations. The first co-design research explores the development of efficient chiral quantum light-matter interactions by co-optimizing defect single photon emission from two-dimensional material based heterostructures and manufacturable integrated photonic components. The second co-design research explores the development of resource-efficient integrated quantum photonic circuit synthesis that considers physical photonic devices limitations. A workshop on quantum materials is held at the University at Buffalo and two workshops on photonic devices and circuits are held at the University of Utah. Through the broad participation of researchers, educators, and students, these workshops facilitate the development of research plans and broad collaborations for future large research and education projects. This award reflects NSF's statutory mission and has been deemed worthy of support through evaluation using the Foundation's intellectual merit and broader impacts review criteria.

PROTOTYPE OPTIMIZATION ALGORITHMS AND SAMPLING METHODS FOR DIFFERENT FORMULATIONS OF RISK ADVERSE OPTIMIZATION PROBLEMS.  MILESTONE 10.1 (12 MONTHS):  DEMONSTRATE RELATIVE ADVANTAGES OF OPTIMIZATION FORMULATIONS AND SAMPLING STRATEGIES FOR DIFFERENT RISK MEASURES ON PROBLEMS RELATED TO TEST CASE, SUCH AS PROBLEMS GOVERNED BY SEMILINEAR CONVECTION DIFFUSION REACTION SYSTEMS. SUB-TASK 10.2  DEVELOP OPTIMIZATION FRAMEWORK AND CONVERGENCE THEORY TO INCLUDE ADAPTIVE SAMPLING STRATEGIES.  MILESTONES 10.2 (18 MONTHS): DEMONSTRATE ORDER OF MAGNITUDE PERFORMANCE INCREASE DUE TO ADAPTIVE SAMPLING IN EXAMPLES CONSIDERED IN SUB-TASK 10.2.  DEMONSTRATE APPLICABILITY TO DESIGN/CONTROL FORMULATIONS IN JET FLAME TEST PROBLEM. SUB-TASK 11.1  FORMULATE OPTIMAL DESIGN AND CONTROL AS MULTISTAGE DECISION PROBLEMS.  MILESTONE 11.1 (18 MONTHS): FORMLATE THE MULTISTAGE DESIGN PROBLEM; ILLUSTRATE HOW MODELING APPROACH LEADS TO DIFFERENT DECISIONS COMPARED TO A CONVENTIONAL SINGLE-STAGE APPROACH FOR TARGET CONVECTION-DIFFUSION-REACTION MODEL PROBLEMS. SUB-TASK 11.2  EXPLORE TECHNIQUES TO IMPROVE EFFICIENCY FOR MULTISTAGE DESIGN, SUCH AS REDUCED MODELS, DECOMPOSITION AND ITERATIVE METHODS.  MILESTONE 11.2 (27 MONTHS):  DEVISE DECOMPOSITION METHODOLOGIES FOR MULTISTAGE DESIGN PROBLEMS WITH DECISION-DEPENDENT UNCERTAINTIES; TEST ON TARGET CONVECTION-DIFFUSION-REACTION MODEL PROBLES; QUANTIFY THE SCALABILITY OF THE APPROACH WITH RESPECT TO THE DIMENSION OF THE DECISION SPACE COMPARED TO EXISTING APPROACHES THAT DO NOT EMPLOY DECOMPOSITION. SUB-TASK 11.3  RISK AND ROBUSTNESS IN OPTIMIZATION.  MILESTONE 11.3 (36 MONTHS): APPLICATION OF DISTRIBUTIONALLY ROBUST OPTIMIZATION TO THE MULTISTAGE COMBUSTION DESIGN PROBLEMS FORMULATED IN TASK 11.1, DEMONSTRATING DESIGNS THAT IMPROVE BOTH PERFORMANCE AND ROBUSTNESS OVER CONVENTIONAL SINGLE-STAGE DESIGN APPROACHES.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: ADVANCING COMMERCIALIZATION OF CELLULOSE NANOMATERIALS IV FY2018
subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
THIS PROJECT WILL DEVELPOP CELLULOSE NANOCRYSTAL (CNC)-AGRO-PROTEIN NANOCOMPOSITE COATINGS THAT CAN BE APPLIED ONTO THE SURFACE OF PERISHABLE FRUIT (I.E., AVOCADO) TO EXTEND ITS SHELF-LIFE. IN THE SUBRECIPIENT'S PREVIOUS WORK, WE HAVE SHOWN THAT CNC REINFORCED EGG-DERIVED PROTEIN COMPOSITE COATING CAN SIGNIFICANTLY EXTEND THE SHELF LIFE AND COSMETIC APPEARANCE OF VARIOUS FRUITS, INCLUDING BANANA, AVOCADO, STRAWBERRY, AND PAPAYA. THE COATING DEVELOPED IN THE LABORATORY WAS MECHANICALLY ROBUST, EASY TO PRODUCE, AND COULD BE SOURCED FROM WASTE BIOMATERIALS.
THE SUBRECIPIENT'S COATING DEMONSTRATED EXCELLENT ANTIMICROBIAL, GAS, AND MOISTURE BARRIER PROPERTIES AND HAS ACHIEVED SIGNIFICANT MEDIA ATTENTION OWING TO ITS ECO-FRIENDLY, BIODEGRADABLE, WASHABLE, AND EDIBLE NATURE. HOWEVER, TO FULLY UNDERSTAND THE TECHNOLOGICAL VIABILITY OF COMMERCIALIZING THE PROPOSED COATING, THE SUBRECIPIENT SEEKS TO EXPLORE THE OPTIMUM COMBINATIONS OF CNC WITH VARIOUS AGRO-PROTEIN SOURCES, CHEMICAL MODIFICATIONS VIA PLASTICIZERS AND CROSSLINKERS, AND EASILY SCALABLE MECHANISMS FOR CONTROLLED COATING METHODS. SPECIFICALLY, STUDYING THE OPTIMUM COMBINATION OF CNC ALONG WITH AGRO-PROTEINS SUCH AS SOY AND CORN THAT WILL BE CHEMICALLY MODIFIED USING A SUITABLE CONTENT OF NATURAL PLASTICIZER (GLYCEROL) AND CROSSLINKER (CURCUMIN, RIBOFLAVIN) TO FABRICATE THE BIO-NANOCOMPOSITE COATING. THIS STUDY FOCUSES ON AGRO-PROTEINS OWING TO THE CONCERNS OF HUMAN ALLERGIC REACTIONS TO FOOD CONTAINING EGG OR EGG PRODUCTS AND A PORTION OF THE WORLD POPULATION STRICTLY FOLLOWING A VEGETARIAN DIET. IN ORDER TO ASSESS THE FINANCIAL VIABILITY, THE SUBRECIPIENT PROPOSES TO CARRY OUT THE TECHNO-ECONOMIC ANALYSIS OF OUR INVENTION. OVERALL, WITH AN END GOAL OF IMPROVING THE ENVIRONMENTAL PERFORMANCE AND SUSTAINABILITY OF THE FOOD INDUSTRY, THE OUTCOMES OF OUR PROPOSAL WILL LEAD TO THE DEVELOPMENT OF A CNC-AGRO-PROTEIN BASED INEXPENSIVE AND GREEN COATING THAT WILL ENHANCE THE SHELF-LIFE OF AVOCADO WITH THE ADDED BENEFITS OF BIODEGRADABILITY, WASHABILITY, EDIBILITY, AND COSMETIC APPEARANCE AND PROVIDE A BUSINESS FEASIBILITY ANALYSIS FOR THE POTENTIAL COMMERCIALIZATION OF THE PRODUCT.

subaward_description: THE LONG TERM OF THIS PROJECT IS TO PREVENT AND TREAT IBC BY TARGETING CELLULAR AND MOLECULAR CHANGES IN THE BREAST TISSUE THAT REGULATE BREAST STEM CELLS TO CREATE A PRE-MALIGNANT FIELD EFFECT. THE OBJECTIVE OF THIS APPLICATION WHICH IS THE NEXT STEP PURSUANT TO THIS GOAL IS TO DEMONSTRATE THAT ALTERED MACROPHAGE NUMBER AND FUNCTION IN IBC HAVE ACTIVATED/POLARIZED MESENCHYMAL STROMAL CELLS (MSCS) INTO "MACROPHAGE EDUCATED MSCS" WHICH CONTRIBUTE TO PROTECTIVE PRE-METASTATIC NICHES WITHIN THE BREAST INTO WHICH EVENTUAL BREAST CANCER STEM CELLS HOME.

subaward_description: COMPLETE THE DEVELOPMENT OF THE GIARDIA DETECTION ASSAY AS WELL AS THE DETECTION ASSAYS FOR THE OTHER LEADING PROTOZOAN CAUSES OF DIARRHEA. WE PLAN TO INTEGRATE THE ASSAYS INTO A SINGLE, MULTIPLEXED ASSAY THAT CAN DIFFERENTIATE BETWEEN THE LEADING CAUSES OF CHRONIC DIARRHEA. WE WILL OPTIMIZE THE REACTION CONDITIONS FOR THE MULTIPLEXED ASSAY INCLUDING PRIMER CONCENTRATIONS, REACTION TIME, AND REACTION TEMPERATURE. WE WILL ALSO DEDICATE SIGNIFICANT TIME AND FOCUS TO DEVELOPING A SAMPLE PREPARATION STRATEGY THAT IS MORE AMENABLE TO THE POINT-OF-CARE. IDEALLY, THE SAMPLE PREPARATION STRATEGY CAN BE INTEGRATED WITH THE AMPLIFICATION AND DETECTION PLATFOREMS TO CREATE A SINGLE SAMPLE-TO-ANSWER DEVICE.

subaward_description:
DR. GENEVERA ALLEN WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR DEVELOPING AND TESTING GRAPHICAL MODELS FOR INFERRING
NEURONAL CONNECTIVITY FROM LARGE POPULATION RECORDINGS. IN THIS ENDEAVOR, SHE WILL SUPERVISE
GRADUATE STUDENTS AND WORK COLLABORATIVELY WITH OTHER INVESTIGATORS TO ENSURE THAT ALL DEVELOPED
MODELS ARE APPROPRIATE FOR NEURAL CONNECTIVITY. SHE WILL SPECIFICALLY PROPOSE NEW GRAPHICAL
MODELS, DEVELOP ESTIMATORS THAT CAN BE APPLIED TO LARGE-SCALE POPULATIONS, AND STUDY THE
THEORETICAL STATISTICAL PROPERTIES OF THE MODELS AND ESTIMATORS TO PROVIDE PROBABILISTIC GUARANTEES
FOR NETWORK RECOVERY. FINALLY, SHE WILL WORK SYNERGISTICLY WITH OTHER INVESTIGATORS THROUGH JOINT LAB
MEETINGS AND WORKSHOPS.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: RINGS: LARA: Layering for Active Resiliency and Awareness in Next-generation Wireless Networks -Wireless networks are an essential societal infrastructure, much like power, water, and transportation systems. With the growing incidence of natural and human-induced disruptions, it has become critical that network service is resilient to unforeseen disruptive events. The state of the art in wireless network design does not directly provision for large-scale disruptive events such as a weather event disabling base-stations. In other words, the wireless networks of today play a small role in ensuring its own resiliency. Our research advances the national infrastructure, prosperity and welfare by providing a novel resiliency paradigm for Next Generation networks. In particular, the specific aim of this research is to design a new generation of wireless networks that stay vigilant by sensing both the internal system state and the external environment. The inclusion of sensing into a future physical layer will be a cornerstone of realizing next generation (such as 6G) wireless networks. Joint sensing and communication will enable high performance allocation of limited resources and create the ability for the network to reason about itself and its environment. We assert that such a paradigm needs to adopt the following design principles: First and foremost, resilient systems assume that unforeseen disruptions can and will occur. Second, the systems are smart in their vigilance by actively managing the resources to form a world-view that enables active preparedness and resilience. Third, armed with active vigilance, resilient systems are in a constant state of preparedness, continually adapting their view of the world by counterfactually reasoning about the system?s future. This project will facilitate the translation of academic research through an active and sustained engagement with industry on this future network design. The proposed work will yield software, open data sharing and research leveraging the PIs? extensive track-record of open-source experiments. The PIs will expand the existing educational and outreach programs to increase participation from under-represented groups in Electrical & Computer Engineering at their institutions. These programs focus on all levels of education and engagement, from faculty to post-doctoral researchers, graduate students and undergraduates. In particular, we will take a data-driven approach to widening participation of underrepresented groups in flagship conferences of the field as well as the annual Information Theory and Applications workshop which is held annually at University of California San Diego. This award reflects NSF's statutory mission and has been deemed worthy of support through evaluation using the Foundation's intellectual merit and broader impacts review criteria.

THE MAIN OBJECTIVE OF OUR PROPOSED RESEARCH IS A SYSTEMATIC INTEGRATION OF CONTEXT-SENSING IN A
NETWORK STACK. CONTEXT-SENSING CONSISTS OF SENSING BOTH PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT USING SENSORS AND
THE LOGICAL NETWORK ENVIRONMENT.
IN THIS PROPOSAL, OUR SPECIFIC RESEARCH AIM IS TO RETHINK WIRELESS NETWORKING WITH REGARDS TO
PROVISIONING AND UTILIZING RISK-MODULATED CONTEXTUAL AWARENESS/SENSING. WE ARGUE THAT WHILE
NETWORK-WIDE CONTEXTUAL AWARENESS/SENSING IS AN ADDITIONAL NETWORK PRIMITIVE, NOT CURRENTLY
PROVISIONED IN A NATIVE MANNER AS PART OF THE NETWORKING PROTOCOL STACK, PROVISIONING IT ACROSS A
NETWORK NOT ONLY WOULD PROVIDE A SLEW OF NEW NETWORKING APPLICATION SERVICES IT WOULD ALSO
ENABLES A HIGHER DEGREE OF RESILIENCY. FOR INSTANCE, IN THE CONTEXT OF THE DRONE NETWORK EXAMPLE,
THE DRONES WILL NOT ONLY CARRY SENSORS LIKE CAMERAS BUT CAN ALSO USE THEIR WIRELESS TRANSCEIVERS FOR
OPERATIONS LIKE RADAR. COLLECTIVELY THE SENSING ARCHITECTURE, THAT CO-EXISTS AND IS CO-OPTIMIZED
WITH NETWORKING, CAN OPEN APPLICATION POSSIBILITIES THAT ARE NOT POSSIBLE TODAY. FURTHERMORE, THE
INTEGRATION OF CONTEXT AWARENESS/SENSING WOULD ALLOW THE DRONES TO CONTINUALLY ADAPT TRANCEIVER
SETTING, RE(ARRANGE) DRONES' POSITIONING, AND/OR (RE)CONFIGURE THE NETWORKING PROTOCOLS AGAINST
CHANGES IN THE ENVIRONMENT, LINK ANOMALIES, AND NETWORK FAILURES.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: LIFE IS ELECTRIC. THE ELECTRONIC CIRCUITRY IS CATALYZED BY A SMALL SUBSET OF PROTEINS THAT FUNCTION AS SOPHISTICATED NANOMACHINES. CURRENTLY  VERY LITTLE IS KNOWN ABOUT THE ORIGIN OF PROTEINS ON EARTH AND THEIR EVOLUTION IN EARLY MICROBIAL LIFE. TO FILL THIS KNOWLEDGE GAP  THE ENIGMA RESEARCH PROGRAM WILL CARRY OUT EXPERIMENTAL  BIOINFORMATIC  DATA-DRIVEN ABDUCTIVE STUDIES TO EXPLORE THE ORIGIN OF CATALYSIS  THE EVOLUTION OF PROTEIN STRUCTURES IN MICROBIAL ANCESTORS  AND THE CO-EVOLUTION OF PROTEINS AND THE GEOSPHERE THROUGH GEOLOGIC TIME. ENIGMA HAS THREE INTEGRATED RESEARCH THEMES FOCUSED ON UNDERSTANDING THE EVOLUTION OF PROTEINS INVOLVED IN ELECTRON TRANSFER AND ENERGY GENERATION. THEME 1 WILL INVESTIGATE THE SYNTHESIS AND FUNCTION OF METALLOPROTEINS IN PREBIOTIC SYSTEMS. EXTANT MICROBIAL PROTEINS WILL BE ANALYZED TO IDENTIFY THE SIMPLEST STRUCTURES THAT CAN PERFORM ELECTRON TRANSFER REACTIONS. USING DE NOVO COMPUTATIONAL PROTEIN DESIGN  SMALL PEPTIDES WILL BE SYNTHESIZED AND CHARACTERIZED TO ELUCIDATE THE EARLIEST BIOCHEMICAL REACTIONS IN THE ORIGIN OF LIFE. THEME 2 WILL EXAMINE THE EMERGENT COMPLEXITY OF METALLOPROTEINS IN MICROBIAL ANCESTORS. NEW COMPUTATIONAL METHODS LINKING PROTEIN STRUCTURE AND PHYLOGENY WILL BE DEVELOPED TO STUDY THE EVOLUTION OF METAL BINDING LIGANDS IN PROTEINS. A STRUCTURAL ALIGNMENT-BASED HOMOLOGY APPROACH WILL BE USED TO IDENTIFY THE OLDEST STRUCTURES IN PROTEINS AND TO TRACE THE EARLY EVOLUTION OF METAL BINDING FOLDS. THEME 3 WILL EXPLORE THE CO-EVOLUTION OF PROTEINS AND THE GEOSPHERE. DEEP-TIME DATA ON THE NATURE AND DISTRIBUTION OF REDOX-SENSITIVE ELEMENTS IN ROCKS AND MINERALS WILL BE INTEGRATED WITH PROTEIN STRUCTURAL DATA IN THE PROTEIN DATA BANK. MINERALOGICAL DATA (E.G. MINERAL COMPOSITION  SOLUBILITY  AND AGE) WILL BE INCORPORATED INTO GEOCHEMICAL MODELS TO CONSTRAIN THE BIOAVAILABILITY OF METALS OVER GEOLOGIC TIME AND METAL BIOAVAILABILITY DURING ARCHEAN AND PROTEROZOIC ERAS WILL USED TO DATE SPECIFIC POSITIONS IN THE EVOLUTIONARY TREE CONSTRUCTED IN THEME 2. THE THREE ENIGMA RESEARCH THEMES ARE DIRECTLY RELEVANT TO THE MAJOR TOPICS  SYNTHESIS AND FUNCTION OF MACROMOLECULES IN THE ORIGIN OF LIFE    EARLY LIFE AND INCREASING COMPLEXITY  AND  CO-EVOLUTION OF LIFE AND THE PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT  IDENTIFIED IN THE 2015 ASTROBIOLOGY SCIENCE STRATEGY. FURTHERMORE  THE ENIGMA PROGRAM COMPLEMENTS THE NAI CAN 7 TEAMS ON EXPERIMENTAL MICROBIAL GENOMICS (GEORGIA TECH) AND LIFE ON A DYNAMIC EARLY EARTH (UC RIVERSIDE). THE RESULTS OF THE ENIGMA RESEARCH PROGRAM WILL HELP THE NAI ANSWER A CENTRAL QUESTION IN ASTROBIOLOGY: HOW DID PROTEINS EVOLVE TO BECOME THE CATALYSTS OF LIFE ON EARTH?

subaward_description: AT RICE UNIVERSITY, DR. DUENAS-OSORIO WILL LEAD THE ASPECTS OF TASK 2.1 FOCUSED ON UNCOVERING AND CATALOGUING THE STRUCTURE OF REAL-WORLD INTERDEPENDENT NETWORKS. HE WILL ALSO LEAD TASK 3.1 ON TRANSLATING ANECDOTAL LESSONS ON MULTI-MODAL, DISTRIBUTED RECOVERY OF INTERDEPENDENT NETWORKS INTO SYSTEMATIC, RIGOROUS UNDERSTANDING. HE WILL COLLABORATE CLOSELY WITH DRS. D'SOUZA AND MESBAHI IN TASK 2.3 ON DEVELOPING NEW MATHEMATICAL REPRESENTATIONS, ENSURING THE STRUCTURES CAPTURE IMPORTANT FEATURES IN REAL-WORLD INTERDEPENDENT NETWORKS. HE WILL CONTRIBUTE SUBSTANTIALLY TO THE ASPECTS OF TASK 1.1 FOCUSED ON RECONFIGURABILITY, TASK 1.2 FOCUSED ON ENERGETIC AND COMPUTATIONAL DEMANDS REQUIRED BY DIFFERENT CONTROL MODES, AND TASK 1.3 STRESSING THE STATE DEPENDENT NATURE OF CONTROL. IN ADDITION HE WILL DISSEMINATE RESULTS OF THE WORK CONDUCTED UNDER THIS PROJECT IN TOP RESEARCH JOURNALS AND CONFERENCES IN THE FIELD, HELP ORGANIZE AND ATTEND WORKSHOPS AND SEMINARS AT MAJOR CONFERENCES AND SCIENTIFIC VENUES TO CREATE A COMMUNITY OF RESEARCHERS FOCUSED ON CONTROL OF NETWORKS, AND GUIDE THE TRAINING AND PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT OF STUDENTS AND POSTDOCTORAL RESEARCHERS INVOLVED IN THIS PROJECT.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: THIS PROJECT ADDRESSES THE HEALTH AND AIR QUALITY APPLICATION AREA, AND ITS PURPOSE IS TO IMPROVE THE REPRESENTATION OF THE PHYSICAL ATMOSPHERE IN AI
subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
1.	EXTEND IMPLEMENTATION OF THE BERKELEY DALHOUSIE SOIL NOX PARAMETERIZATION (BDSNP) FROM CMAQ INTO THE STAND-ALONE MEGAN BIOGENIC EMISSIONS MODEL TO: 
A.	OPTIONALLY UTILIZE SATELLITE DATA FOR SOLAR RADIATION, SOIL MOISTURE, AND SOIL TEMPERATURE;
B.	UTILIZE EPIC FERTILIZER DATA, FOR DYNAMIC YEAR-SPECIFIC REPRESENTATION OF NITROGEN APPLICATIONS TO SOILS.

2.	EVALUATE THE IMPACT OF SATELLITE DERIVED SOIL MOISTURE, TEMPERATURE, AND IMPROVED MODEL METEOROLOGY ON MEGAN ESTIMATES OF VEGETATIVE EMISSIONS OF VOCS AND SOIL EMISSIONS OF NO.

3.	UTILIZE THE BENMAP MODEL TO CHARACTERIZE THE HEALTH EFFECTS OF THE BIOGENIC EMISSIONS SIMULATED IN THIS WORK. CONCENTRATION-RESPONSE FUNCTIONS WILL BE SELECTED FROM EXISTING BENEFITS ANALYSES BY BRYAN HUBBELL AND OTHERS AT US EPA. ALSO, USE NEW IMPROVED CONCENTRATION RESPONSE FUNTIONS FOR OSZONE AND PM2.5 IN BENMAP. WORKING WITH OTHER SCIENTISTS AT UAH EVALUATE THE MODIFIED MODEL.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: Controllable In Vivo Genome Editing for Immune-Checkpoint Blockade in Solid Tumors
prime_award_cfda_numbers_and_titles: 93.286: DISCOVERY AND APPLIED RESEARCH FOR TECHNOLOGICAL INNOVATIONS TO IMPROVE HUMAN HEALTH
subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
THE PROPOSED SPECIFIC AIMS FOR THE RESEARCH TEAM ARE OF THE FOLLOWING: AIM 1. DESIGN AND CHARACTERIZE MNP-BV-CRISPR SYSTEMS FOR IN VIVO CRISPR/CAS9 DELIVERY. AIM 2. prime_award_cfda_numbers_and_titles: 93.286: DISCOVERY AND APPLIED RESEARCH FOR TECHNOLOGICAL INNOVATIONS TO IMPROVE HUMAN HEALTH
subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
THE PROPOSED SPECIFIC AIMS FOR THE RESEARCH TEAM ARE OF THE FOLLOWING: AIM 1. DESIGN AND CHARACTERIZE MNP-BV-CRISPR SYSTEMS FOR IN VIVO CRISPR/CAS9 DELIVERY. AIM 2. DETERMINE THE GENE EDITING EFFICIENCY, SPECIFICITY AND FUNCTIONAL EFFECTS OF MNP-BV-CRISPR IN VITRO. AIM 3. DETERMINE THE EFFECTS OF MNP-BV-CRISPR ON IMMUNE-CHECKPOINT BLOCKADE IN VIVO.
THE BAO LAB WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR QUANTIFYING THE EFFICIENCY OF MULTIPLEXED GENE DISRUPTION OF MNP-BV-CRISPR INCLUDING THE ON- AND OFF-TARGET GENE EDITING RATES, AND QUANTIFYING MNP-BV-CRISPR INDUCED GENE DISRUPTION IN TUMOR TISSUE.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: THIS PROJECT ADDRESSES THE HEALTH AND AIR QUALITY APPLICATION AREA, AND ITS PURPOSE IS TO IMPROVE THE REPRESENTATION OF THE PHYSICAL ATMOSPHERE IN AI
subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
1.	EXTEND IMPLEMENTATION OF THE BERKELEY DALHOUSIE SOIL NOX PARAMETERIZATION (BDSNP) FROM CMAQ INTO THE STAND-ALONE MEGAN BIOGENIC EMISSIONS MODEL TO: 
A.	OPTIONALLY UTILIZE SATELLITE DATA FOR SOLAR RADIATION, SOIL MOISTURE, AND SOIL TEMPERATURE;
B.	UTILIZE EPIC FERTILIZER DATA, FOR DYNAMIC YEAR-SPECIFIC REPRESENTATION OF NITROGEN APPLICATIONS TO SOILS.

2.	EVALUATE THE IMPACT OF SATELLITE DERIVED SOIL MOISTURE, TEMPERATURE, AND IMPROVED MODEL METEOROLOGY ON MEGAN ESTIMATES OF VEGETATIVE EMISSIONS OF VOCS AND SOIL EMISSIONS OF NO.

3.	UTILIZE THE BENMAP MODEL TO CHARACTERIZE THE HEALTH EFFECTS OF THE BIOGENIC EMISSIONS SIMULATED IN THIS WORK. CONCENTRATION-RESPONSE FUNCTIONS WILL BE SELECTED FROM EXISTING BENEFITS ANALYSES BY BRYAN HUBBELL AND OTHERS AT US EPA. ALSO, USE NEW IMPROVED CONCENTRATION RESPONSE FUNTIONS FOR OSZONE AND PM2.5 IN BENMAP. WORKING WITH OTHER SCIENTISTS AT UAH EVALUATE THE MODIFIED MODEL.

subaward_description:
WE WILL ENGINEER THE NTRAIN SYSTEM, A WIRELESSLY CONTROLLED BIOELECTRONIC/SYNTHETIC BIOLOGY
HYBRID IMPLANTED DEVICE THAT WILL REDUCE THE TIME NEEDED TO ADAPT TO NEW TIMEZONES OR DRASTIC
CHANGES IN WORK SCHEDULES. NTRAIN WILL ACCURATELY CONTROL THE PRODUCTION OF NATIVE BIOLOGICS BY
ENGINEERED CELL FACTORIES WHICH WILL BE TRIGGERED AND REGULATED OPTICALLY VIA THE IMPLANTED
BIOELECTRONIC PLATFORM. THIS LIVING PHARMACY WILL BE SUPPORTED BY A BIOELECTRONIC CARRIER WHICH
WILL SUPPORT AND ENHANCE CELL VIABILITY AND BIOLOGICS PRODUCTION. THE IMPLANT, DEPLOYED
SUBCUTANEOUSLY, WILL BE WIRELESSLY POWERED BY MAGNETO-ELECTRIC TRANSDUCERS AND TRANSMIT DATA BY
NEAR FIELD COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS TO A LOW PROFILE WEARABLE HUB. THE HUB WILL COMMUNICATE
EITHER DIRECTLY OR VIA A CUSTOM SMARTPHONE APPLICATION IN ORDER TO DIRECT THE WARFIGHTER TO TRIGGER
THE DELIVERY OF PEPTIDE THERAPEUTICS PRODUCED BY THE MAMMALIAN CELL FACTORIES. THIS PLATFORM FORMS
THE BASIS FOR A LIVING BIOELECTRONIC PHARMACY. THE IMPLANTABLE NTRAIN DEVICE IS MEANT TO BE
DEPLOYED VIA OUTPATIENT SUBCUTANEOUS IMPLANTATION, AND BE VIABLE FOR AT LEAST 60 DAYS, BUT IS BASED
ON TECHNOLOGY THAT ENABLES LONG TERM VIABILITY, WITH TRIGGERED DELIVERY POSSIBLE DURING ITS LIFETIME.
SURGICAL DEVICE EXPLANTATION OR ADMINISTRATION OF APPROVED SMALL MOLECULE DRUGS WHICH ACTIVATE A
KILL SWITCH IN THE CELL FACTORIES RENDER THE DEVICE INACTIVE. COMPONENT (CELL, COMMUNICATION, ETC.)
DEVELOPMENT WILL BE VALIDATED IN RODENT MODELS, WITH THE INTEGRATED BIOELECTRONIC AND HYBRID
SYSTEM TESTED FOR SAFETY AND EFFICACY IN NON-HUMAN PRIMATE (NHP) MODELS. WE HAVE ELECTED TO USE
NHPS DUE TO THEIR HIGH TRANSLATIONAL VALIDITY TO HUMAN SLEEP-WAKE STATES AND CIRCADIAN RHYTHM
PATTERNS, AS WELL AS IMPROVED CELL VIABILITY RESPONSE OF ALLOGENEIC OPTICALLY-INDICIBLE CELL-BASED
FACTORIES AND THEIR DELIVERED NATIVE PEPTIDES, AND WE HAVE MADE CONCERTED EFFORTS TO REDUCE THE
QUANTITY OF NHPS NEEDED (APPENDIX A). BIOELECTRONIC CARRIER/COMMUNICATION COMPONENTS WILL BE
TESTED FOR SAFETY IN PHASE 3 FIRST IN HUMAN CLINICAL TRIAL.
THE MECHANISTIC BASIS THAT WILL ENABLE A 50% REDUCTION IN ENTRAINMENT TIME IS THE TARGETING
OF BOTH CENTRAL AND PERIPHERAL CIRCADIAN CLOCKS AND MITIGATING THEIR DESYNCHRONY. WE WILL EMPLOY
NON-TRADITIONAL PEPTIDE-BASED THERAPIES GROUNDED IN SLEEP AND CIRCADIAN SCIENCE LITERATURE FOR
PRODUCTION BY CELL FACTORIES. WE WILL TARGET PEPTIDES WHICH WILL ACT ON BOTH THE CENTRAL
(HYPOTHALAMIC SUPRACHIASMATIC NUCLEUS) CLOCK AND ON PERIPHERAL CLOCKS IN CONCERT (INITIAL TARGETS:
GLUCAGON-LIKE PEPTIDE-1, GLP-1; OREXIN-A), WITH OTHERS NOTED IN RISK MITIGATION IN VOLUME I.
THESE THERAPIES ARE EXPECTED TO ACT SYNERGISTICALLY BY ACTING ON PATHWAYS ENGAGED IN SLEEP-WAKE
AND FEED-FAST CYCLES. QUANTIFYING THE TIMING OF THERAPEUTIC RELEASE WITH RESPECT TO CLOCK TIME, AS

ADAPTATION TO A NEW TIME ZONE OR SCHEDULE FOR WARFIGHTERS, SHIFT WORKERS, AND FIRST RESPONDERS, IN

prime_award_base_transaction_description: Western Regional Noyce Network -The project aims to serve the national need of supporting STEM teachers as collaborators towards advancing teaching and learning and persisting in the teaching profession. The national teacher shortage was exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic, with STEM teaching positions among the hardest to fill. Equity gaps have worsened, and teacher diversity does not come near to reflecting the diversity of the student population. Meanwhile, expectations for teachers? content and pedagogical knowledge have increased. Given this context, the goals of the Robert Noyce Teacher Scholarship Program have never been more urgent. While each institution with a Noyce project works individually to recruit, prepare, and retain STEM teachers, this project supports a larger community that works collectively to enhance and magnify efforts to increase the quality preparation of Noyce recipients while reinforcing their commitment to teaching careers. Known as the Western Regional Noyce Network (WRNN), this project builds on the success of ongoing efforts to foster community and support for Noyce recipients and Noyce project leaders. Specifically, in order to motivate teachers to persist and thrive in the teaching profession, this project aims to expand the involvement of Noyce recipients, especially current recipients and recent graduates, and to strengthen their community ties through purposeful activities designed with explicit networking goals. Project activities include (1) annual conferences, (2) professional development offerings, (3) regional networking associated with statewide educator conferences hosted by professional societies, (4) development of an online learning community, and (5) robust formative and impact evaluation. This WRNN project integrates the efforts of 13 institutions across a 14-state region of the western United States. The collaborating institutions are: the University of Oregon (lead) and, alphabetically, California Polytechnic State University, California State University, Fresno, California State University, Northridge, California State University, Sacramento, Pacific University, Planetary Science Institute, Rice University, San Diego State University, San Francisco State University, University of Portland, University of Texas at Arlington, University of Wyoming. It is designed to expand involvement by Noyce projects and their Scholars and Fellows, promote professional networking, foster exchange of ideas among Noyce recipients and program leaders, and facilitate professional development. The WRNN expects to serve approximately 900 participants over three annual conferences, to engage 450 participants in aggregate in professional development opportunities, to support another 665 participants in regional networking events, and the online learning community will be open to thousands. Questions that will be explored center on examining the networks that Noyce Scholars/Fellows and alumni develop as a result of WRNN activities and the benefits that these networks confer. Varied formats of professional development allow for exploration of affordances and constraints of in-person and virtual activities. By promoting networking throughout all activities and offering targeted activities that address identified needs and interests of Noyce teachers, hundreds of thousands of students in high-need schools across the region will benefit beyond the lifetime of this project from highly prepared and well-connected STEM teachers. This Noyce conference project is supported through the Robert Noyce Teacher Scholarship Program (Noyce). The Noyce program supports talented STEM undergraduate majors and professionals to become effective K-12 STEM teachers and experienced, exemplary K-12 teachers to become STEM master teachers in high-need school districts. It also supports research on the effectiveness and retention of K-12 STEM teachers in high-need school districts. This award reflects NSF's statutory mission and has been deemed worthy of support through evaluation using the Foundation's intellectual merit and broader impacts review criteria.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: NSF Convergence Accelerator Track G: PETS: Programmable Zero-Trust Security for Operating Through 5G Infrastructure -5G mobile communication technology has been standardized to provide high-speed Internet connectivity to various devices and support diverse applications/services. The request for enhanced security is becoming a critical issue when users such as DoD want to securely operate through the current 5G infrastructure. The goal of this proposal is to provide the DoD (and any 5G users and operators) with new, flexible, and programmable zero-trust security capability when operating through existing 5G infrastructure. This project provides a solid foundation and collaborative community for existing and future 5G security operation and research. The key broader social impacts and societal benefits include improved economic outputs, safety, security, privacy, cost of regulation, liability, interruption protection, and distribution of access. This multi-institute project includes experts in security, networking/communication, telecom operation, convergence research, public policy, user study, team science, and national security applications from multiple academic institutions (including an HBCU) and industry partners. This project seeks to broaden participation in the convergence accelerator research by engaging with diverse stakeholder groups including private and public organizations, decision makers, and members of the public, particularly under-represented minority groups. Transitioning from existing research collectively done by the team, this project proposes to build an innovative programmable zero-trust security solution called PETS to enable unified, infrastructure-wide, dynamic, and granular flow-level security control across the entire 5G infrastructure, including end devices, 5G Radio Access Network (RAN), and 5G core network. Based on the proposed system, the 5G operators, developers and/or users can easily write security applications to customize their specific security needs and realize zero-trust security features. Such new security applications can continuously maintain and evaluate risks of accesses, and provide finer-grained, programmable access control and isolation of resources in 5G infrastructure. This project allows military, government, or critical infrastructure operators to securely operate through existing 5G infrastructure with respect to either non-cooperative or cooperative networks. It also enables new innovations in programming dynamic and intelligent security applications to protect the entire 5G infrastructure. This award reflects NSF's statutory mission and has been deemed worthy of support through evaluation using the Foundation's intellectual merit and broader impacts review criteria.

DAVID ZHANG AND TWO PERSONNEL IN HIS LABORATORY (PING SONG AND PENG DAI) WILL WORK WITH DR.

ZHANG'S GROUP WILL APPLY MULTIPLEX BDA NGS TO BLOOD AND TUMOR SAMPLES FROM DR. KWONG'S
MOUSE MODELS OF MELANOMA.

subaward_description: METAMATERIALS SHOW POTENTIAL FOR TAILORING THE WAVEFRONT OF THE INITIAL COMPRESSIVE SHOCK AND THE RETURNING TENSILE SHOCKS THROUGH SELECTIVE DEFLECTION, MODE TRANSFORMATION, FOCUSING, OR ENHANCED ENERGY DISSIPATION IN THE STRUCTURES TO MITIGATE SUBSEQUENT DAMAGE OR TO DIRECT THE BLAST WAVE TO A SUBSEQUENT TARGET. AT RICE, THE THOMAS GROUP WILL DESIGN AND MODEL WAVE PROPAGATION AND RESPONSES OF METAMATERIAL STRUCTURES, FABRICATE SOFT/HARD STRUCTURES POLYMER/FLUID NANOFRAME STRUCTURES  THROUGH 3D INTERFERENCE LITHOGRAPHY; AND DIRECTLY VISUALIZE AND MEASURE PROPAGATION OF LASER-GENERATED BLAST WAVES AND SHOCK WAVES IN AND THROUGH THE STRUCTURES.  BECAUSE OUR APPROACH CAN DIRECTLY PROVIDE TIME-RESOLVED IMAGING OF BLAST WAVES ON AN IMPACT REGION SMALLER THAN 1 MM IN DIAMETER, IT ENABLES SYSTEMATIC STUDIES IN A LARGE GROUP OF NANOSTRUCTURED MATERIALS, REVEALING THE INFLUENCE OF LARGE DEFORMATION AND DISTORTION IN THE METAMATERIAL ON WAVE MANIPULATION. THE PROPOSED BASIC RESEARCH WILL YIELD DISCOVERY AND VALIDATION OF RELIABLE MODELING PARADIGMS, SCALABLE METAMATERIAL DESIGNS, AND PROTOTYPE COMPONENTS AND COMPOSITIONS FOR SUPERIOR BLAST WAVE MITIGATION AND CONTROL.

RICE UNIVERSITY BIOSCIENCES AT RICE GRADUATE STUDENT KAITLIN SCHOLAND WILL BE WORKING WITH DR. CINTIA  DE PAIVA AT BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE ON A RESEARCH PROJECT INVOLVING SJÃƑÆ’Ã‚Â¶GREN SYNDROME. SJÃƑÆ’Ã‚Â¶GREN SYNDROME (SS) IS AN AUTOIMMUNE DISORDER PRIMARILY AFFECTING WOMEN. THE EXOCRINE GLANDS ARE INVOLVED, INCLUDING THE LACRIMAL AND SALIVARY GLANDS. PATIENTS CAN HAVE DRY EYE, DRY MOUTH, OR A COMBINATION OF BOTH DUE TO ACTIVATED IMMUNE CELLS THAT INFILTRATE BOTH ORGANS. CD4+ T CELLS AND B CELLS INVADE AND ATTACK SECRETORY ACINAR CELLS, ALTHOUGH SOME STUDIES SUGGEST THAT ALTERED EPITHELIAL CELL BIOLOGY COULD PRECEDE THE INFILTRATION OF IMMUNE CELLS. THE GOAL OF MS. SCHOLANDÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S RESEARCH IS TO
BETTER ELUCIDATE THE GENETIC MARKERS OF SS, IDENTIFY THE PROTECTIVE MECHANISM OF THE MICROBIOME ON SS DISEASE, AND INVESTIGATE HOW TREGS ACT IN SS PATIENTS. THIS WORK CAN LEAD TO AN ACCURATE, NONINVASIVE DIAGNOSTIC TEST AND HIGHLIGHT POTENTIAL MARKERS FOR TREATMENTS.

DAVID ZHANG AND TWO PERSONNEL IN HIS LABORATORY (PING SONG AND PENG DAI) WILL WORK WITH DR.
LAWRENCE KWONG AT UT MD ANDERSON TO DEVELOP NOVEL TECHNOLOGIES TO DETECT AND QUANTITATE
RARE DRUG RESISTANCE MUTATIONS IN HISTORICAL MELANOMA PATIENT TISSUE SAMPLES. THE TECHNOLOGY
BEING DEVELOPED IS NAMED MULTIPLEX BDA NGS, AND WILL BE CAPABLE OF DETECTING AND QUANTITATING
RARE DRUG RESISTANCE MUTATIONS WITH DOWN TO 0.01% MUTATION FREQUENCY, ACROSS ANY OF ROUGHLY
100 CANCER HOTSPOTS. ROUGHLY 200 SAMPLES WILL BE ANALYZED OVER THE COURSE OF TWO YEARS. EACH
OF THE SAMPLES WILL BE PROVIDED DE-IDENTIFIED TO DR. ZHANG'S RESEARCH GROUP. ADDITIONALLY, DR.
ZHANG'S GROUP WILL APPLY MULTIPLEX BDA NGS TO BLOOD AND TUMOR SAMPLES FROM DR. KWONG'S
MOUSE MODELS OF MELANOMA.

AIM 1. TO TEST THE HYPOTHESIS THAT PYROLYSIS WILL EFFICIENTLY AND BROADLY REMOVE PAHS AND OTHER HYDROPHOBIC PRIORITY POLLUTANTS PRESENT IN SUPERFUND SITE SEDIMENTS AND SOILS, AND ELIMINATE THEIR TOXICITY.
AIM 2. CHARACTERIZE THE REACTION MECHANISMS AND END PRODUCTS TO GUIDE SAFE, RELIABLE AND COST-EFFICIENT APPLICATION. SPECIFICALLY, WE WILL USE THERMOGRAVIMETRY AND EVOLVED GAS ANALYSES TO ELUCIDATE THE PHYSICAL AND CHEMICAL PROCESSES OCCURRING DURING PYROLYSIS. THE POSSIBLE CATALYTIC EFFECTS OF SOIL COMPONENTS LIKE CLAYS WILL BE SYSTEMATICALLY STUDIED AND SURFACE ANALYSIS TECHNIQUES WILL BE USED TO DETERMINE THE CHEMICAL COMPOSITION AND DISPERSION OF PYROLYSIS PRODUCTS. FINALLY, WE WILL CAREFULLY CHARACTERIZE THE TREATED SEDIMENTS TO DETERMINE HOW KEY PROPERTIES ARE AFFECTED BY THE CHOSEN PYROLYSIS CONDITIONS TO INFORM REACTION MECHANISMS AND GUIDE REACTOR OPTIMIZATION EFFORTS. 
AIM 3. IDENTIFY THE OPERATING CONDITIONS THAT MAXIMIZE THE BENEFITS OF SOIL PYROLYSIS WHILE MINIMIZING ASSOCIATED COSTS. WHEREAS ENSURING A MINIMUM TREATMENT INTENSITY IS CRITICAL FOR EFFICIENT REMEDIATION AND DETOXIFICATION, THERE IS A MAXIMUM INTENSITY BEYOND WHICH PYROLYTIC TREATMENT BECOMES DETRIMENTAL WITH REGARDS TO SOIL DAMAGE AND EXCESSIVE ENERGY USAGE. ACCORDINGLY, OPTIMIZATION WILL ENTAIL LOWERING BOTH PYROLYSIS TIMES. WE WILL ALSO ATTEMPT TO ACHIEVE THE FOLLOWING SUB-AIMS:
A.	ASSESS THE FERTILITY OF PYROLYZED SEDIMENTS BE MEASURING KEY AGRONOMIC PROPERTIES OF THE TREATED SOILS AND COMPARING THEM TO UNTREATED CONTAMINATED SOIL AND CLEAN BACKGROUND SOIL AS CONTROLS. SEED GERMINATION STUDIES WILL ALSO BE USED FOR THIS ASSESSMENT.
B.	ANALYZE THE COMPOSITION OF VOLATILES RELEASED DURING PYROLYSIS, DETERMINE THEIR HEAT CONTENT, AND DECIDE WHETHER THEY SHOULD BE COMBUSTED FOR ENERGY RECOVERY OR TREATED AND DISPOSED.
C.	DEVELOP A PROCESS SIMULATOR TO FORECAST ENERGY REQUIREMENTS AND IMPLEMENTATION COSTS.

PROJECT 5 WILL WORK CLOSELY WITH THE BIOMEDICAL AND ANALYTICAL RESEARCH PROJECTS TO ENSURE EFFECTIVE DETOXIFICATION, HELP DETERMINE ÃƑÂ¯Ã‚Â¿Ã‚Â½HOW CLEAN IS CLEANÃƑÂ¯Ã‚Â¿Ã‚Â½ AND OPTIMIZE OUR TREATMENT PROCESS. EXPECTED BENEFITS OF THE PROPOSED INNOVATION ARE SIGNIFICANT. WE ANTICIPATE THAT PYROLYTIC TREATMENT WILL RAPIDLY AND RELIABLY ACHIEVE REGULATORY COMPLIANCE IN A BROAD RANGE OF SOIL CONTAMINATION SCENARIOS, ENABLE BENEFICIAL DISPOSITION OF THE SOIL, AND CONTRIBUTE A POSITIVE PUBLIC IMAGE, THUS FACILITATING REGULATORY ACCEPTANCE OF THIS NOVEL TECHNOLOGY BY STAKEHOLDERS SUCH AS THE EPA.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: STN DBS Effects on Neuroinflammation and Degeneration Induced by Alpha-Synuclein Inclusions - Project Summary Subthalamic nucleus deep brain stimulation (STN DBS) to treat the cardinal motor symptoms of Parkinson’s disease (PD) has increased dramatically since its first use was reported in 1994. DBS is a vetted, safe and efficacious neurosurgical therapy for PD. Once considered a treatment of last-resort with patients undergoing neurosurgery approximately 10-16 years post diagnosis, STN DBS now is FDA approved for use as early as four years after diagnosis and symptomatic efficacy may be superior to medical therapy at that time. Questions remain as to whom will best benefit from additional and earlier years of stimulation treatment. Specifically, the question of whether early STN DBS can modify the progression of PD has yet to be examined in an appropriately designed clinical trial. Dopaminergic denervation of the putamen is nearly complete within four years of PD diagnosis and precedes loss of nigral neurons. Thus, neuroprotective therapies that seek to protect the nigrostriatal system cannot be adequately evaluated in subjects with disease duration longer than this four-year timeframe. Several clinical studies have investigated whether STN DBS has the ability to slow or halt the progression of PD. However, the common thread in all of these studies is that the subjects enrolled were mid to late-stage PD when STN DBS was initiated. Most recently, a pilot trial has shown that STN DBS was applied within 2 years of PD diagnosis is safe and efficacious with subjects receiving STN DBS exhibiting a slower worsening of rest tremor. This suggests that early DBS may slow some aspects of PD progression. Preclinical studies by our group and others have demonstrated that STN DBS can protect against degeneration of nigrostriatal dopamine (DA) neurons induced by neurotoxicant insult in both rats and nonhuman primates. In our laboratory we have previously shown that STN DBS significantly increases brain- derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) in the nigrostriatal system and the primary motor cortex (M1). Further, we have directly linked the neuroprotective effect of STN DBS to BDNF-tropomyosin receptor kinase type B (trkB) signaling in substantia nigra pars compacta (SNpc) neurons as trkB blockade prevents this neuroprotection. In contrast to results in neurotoxicant models, STN DBS applied in the alpha-synuclein (a-syn) overexpression models has yielded mixed neuroprotection results. Whether STN DBS can protect the nigrostriatal system in the context of synucleinopathy therefore remains an open question. In the present proposal we employ an alternative synucleinopathy model: the a-syn preformed fibril (PFF) model. The a-syn PFF model shares key features of idiopathic PD and may be more disease-relevant to idiopathic PD than a-syn overexpression models, potentially providing greater predictive validity. Using the a-syn PFF model we will determine whether STN DBS can provide neuroprotection of SNpc cell bodies, SNpc nigrostriatal terminals and M1 corticostriatal neurons. We will further investigate the impact of long-term STN DBS on potential arbiters of neuroprotection and disease-modification: neuroinflammation and BDNF.

subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
THE INNER MAGNETOSPHERE SUPPORTS A HOST OF PHYSICAL PROCESSES DRIVING
LOSS, TRANSPORT, AND ACCELERATION OF ENERGETIC PARTICLES IN THE RADIATION BELTS. IN PARTICULAR, UNDERSTANDING THE
PHYSICAL PROCESSES AND TIMESCALES FOR ENERGETIC PARTICLE LOSS ARE CRUCIAL TO UNDERSTANDING THE OVERALL
DYNAMICS OF THE RADIATION BELTS. IN THIS WORK, WE ADDRESS THE OVERARCHING SCIENCE QUESTION: "WHAT ARE THE
RELATIVE EFFECTS OF PARTICLE LOSS DUE TO WAVE-PARTICLE INTERACTIONS AND MAGNETOPAUSE LOSS?" IN PARTICULAR,
1) HOW DO LOSSES DUE TO DIFFERENT MECHANISMS VARY WITH VARYING LEVELS OF SOLAR WIND DRIVERS AND
GEOMAGNETIC ACTIVITY? WE WILL EXAMINE RELEVANT LOSS PROCESSES AS A FUNCTION OF RADIAL LOCATION AND LOCAL
TIME, AND WILL USE EVENT-SPECIFIC SOLAR WIND CONDITIONS AND DIFFUSION MODELS TO CONTRAST PARTICLE LOSS
PROCESSES DURING CME- AND CIR-DRIVEN STORMS.
2) WHAT ARE THE EFFECTS OF DIFFERING ULF AND VLF WAVE DISTRIBUTIONS ON THE RESULTING RADIATION BELT PHASESPACE DENSITY PROFILES? OUR METHOD OF DEVELOPING EVENT-SPECIFIC DIFFUSION MODELS WILL PROVIDE INSIGHT INTO
THE RELATIVE EFFECTS OF THESE WAVES ON RADIATION BELT LOSSES.
3) HOW DO NON-LINEAR WAVE PARTICLE INTERACTIONS CONTRIBUTE AS A POTENTIAL LOSS MECHANISM? OUR
METHODOLOGY CAN BE APPLIED TO BOTH DIFFUSIVE AND ADVECTIVE NONLINEAR WAVE-PARTICLE INTERACTIONS, ALLOWING
QUANTITATIVE COMPARISON WITH QUASILINEAR WPIS.

subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
ABSTRACT
IN THE FIRST FRACTIONS OF A SECOND AFTER THE BIG BANG, THE EXTREMELY ENERGETIC AND DENSE
ENVIRONMENT OF OUR NACENT UNIVERSE WOULD HAVE SOURCED AN INCREDIBLY STRONG GRAVITATIONAL WAVE RADIATION. THIS RADIATION IS EXPECTED OT SURVIVE NI THE UNIVERSE TODAY AS A STOCHASTIC GRAVITATIONAL WAVE BACKGROUND. GRAVITATIONAL WAVE TELESCOPES SUCH AS THE LASER INTERFEROMETER GRAVITATIONAL- WAVE OBSERVATORY (LIGO) ARE CURRENTLY SEARCHING FOR THIS SIGNAL, AND THE UPCOMING LASER INTERFEROMETER SPACE ANTENNA (LISA) WIL EXTEND THE SEARCH BY SEVERAL ORDERS OF MAGNITUDE NI SENSITIVITY. ADETECTION OF THIS PRIMORDIAL GRAVITATIONAL WAVE RADIATION WIL OPEN AWINDOW ONTO THE EARLY UNIVERSE, THERBY ALLOWING US TO EXPLORE THE COMPOSITION AND DYNAMICS OF HTE UNIVERSE AT AN EPOCH THAT REMAINS INACCESSIBLE TO LABORATORY EXPERIMENTS.
WE PROPOSE TO STUDY THE GENERATION OF GRAVITATIONAL WAVE RADIATION FROM VARIOUS EARLY-UNIVERSE
SOURCES. APRIMORDIAL GRAVITATIONAL WAVE BACKGROUND THAT'S DETECTABLE BY LIGO OR LISA CANNOT ARISE FROM THE STANDARD MODEL OF PARTICLE PHYSICS AND COSMOLOGY ALONE, BUT RATHER IT WOULD EB STRONG EVIDENCE FOR NEW PHYSICS. WE PROPOSE TO ANALYZE WELL MOTIVATED MODELS OF AXION-DRIVEN
INFLATION AND COSMOLOGICAL PHASE TRANSITIONS (INCLUDING BUT NOT EXCLUSIVELY FIRST ORDER ONES). WE ARE ESPECIALLY INTERESTED NI THE IMPRINTS OF POSSIBLE PARITY VIOLATION NI THE EARLY UNIVERSE WHICH WILL RESULT IN THE NON-ZERO CIRCULAR POLARIZATION OF THE STOCHASTIC GRAVITATIONAL WAVES BACKGROUND. ANOTHER FOCUS OF OUR RESEARCH WIL CONSISTS ON INVESTIGATING OF PRIMORDIAL PLASMA EFFECTS INCLUDING GENERATION OF PRIMORDIAL PLASMA ACOUSTIC (SOUND WAVES) AND/OR TURBULENT MOTIONS. THE GRAVITATIONAL WAVES FROM PARITY VIOLATING INFLATIONARY SOURCES IMPRINTS ON THE COSMIC MICROWAVE BACKGROUND ANISOTROPIES (BIREFRIGENCE, PARITY-ODD CROSS CORRELATIONS), WHILE THE GRAVITATIONAL WAVES FROM REALISTIC EARLY-UNIVERSE TURBULENT SOURCES POSSIBLY PRESENT AROUND THE ELECTROWEAK ENERGY SCALE ARE WITHIN THE REACH OF LISA SENSITIVITY BOUNDS. WE PROPOSE PHENOMENOLOGICAL AND NUMERICAL STUDIES OF PHYSICAL PROCESSES NI THE EARLY-UNIVERSE OT DETERMINE THE INITIAL CONDITIONS FOR VIABLE GRAVITATIONAL WAVES SOURCES GENERATION AND DEVELOPMENT. IN ADDITION WE WILL SIMULATE THE PLASMA MOTIONS, AND ALSO STUDY THE PROCESS OF MAGNETIC FIELD AMPLIFICATION DURING MAGNETOHYDRODYNAMICAL TURBULENCE.
THE SUCCESSFUL COMPLETION OF THIS WORK WIL FURNISH THE ASTROPHYSICS COMMUNITY WITH (I) ROBUST PREDICTIONS FOR GRAVITATIONAL WAVE SPECTRA FROM EARLY UNIVERSE SOURCES, (II) GUIDANCE FOR DISTINGUISHING DIFFERENT POTENTIAL SOURCES GIVEN THE DATA, AND (ITI) CLEAR UNDERSTANDING OF HOW NEW PHYSICS CAN BE PROBED WITH GRAVITATIONAL WAVE OBSERVATIONS. OVERALL THIS WORK ADVANCES
NASAS' AGENDA OT EXPLORE THE ORIGIN, EVOLUTION, STRUCTURE, AND DESTINY OF THE UNIVERSE ITSELF.

DATASETS AND FULL-PHYSICS GCMS.

"DISTRIBUTED ACOUSTIC SENSING (DAS) IS A RAPIDLY EVOLVING TECHNOLOGY THAT USES FIBER-OPTIC CABLES AS ARRAYS OF SENSORS CAPABLE
OF MEASURING SPATIALLY RESOLVED DYNAMIC STRAIN. DAS HAS THE POTENTIAL TO REVOLUTIONIZE SEISMIC MONITORING BY DRAMATICALLY
INCREASING MEASUREMENT DENSITY OVER LARGE DISTANCES THAT CAN REACH TENS OF KILOMETERS, AND BY MAKING OBSERVATIONS IN
DIVERSE ENVIRONMENTS SUCH AS IN CITIES OR ON THE OCEAN FLOOR, IN WHICH TRADITIONAL SEISMIC MONITORING WOULD BE EITHER
DIFFICULT OR IMPOSSIBLE. DAS DIFFERS FROM CONVENTIONAL SEISMIC MONITORING BECAUSE THE FUNDAMENTAL MEASUREMENT BEARS A
MORE COMPLEX RELATIONSHIP TO THE CAUSATIVE WAVEFIELD. FOR THAT REASON, IT IS ESSENTIAL TO UNDERSTAND HOW IT IS SIMILAR TO,
AND HOW IT DIFFERS FROM, CONVENTIONAL SEISMIC MEASUREMENTS. THE GOAL OF THE PROPOSED PROJECT IS TO DEVELOP A QUANTITATIVE
UNDERSTANDING OF THE POTENTIAL OF DAS FOR SEISMIC MONITORING USING DATA COLLECTED IN A WIDE RANGE OF ENVIRONMENTS.
THE PROPOSED PROGRAM OF RESEARCH WILL SUPPORT A BROAD EFFORT OF LEADING DAS RESEARCHERS AT MULTIPLE INSTITUTIONS WITH THE
AIM OF CARRYING OUT RESEARCH THAT WILL LEAD TO A COMPREHENSIVE UNDERSTANDING OF THE STRENGTHS AND LIMITATIONS OF THIS NEW
TECHNOLOGY SUCH THAT ITS POTENTIAL FOR SEISMIC MONITORING CAN BE FULLY REALIZED. IN YEAR 1, THE PROJECT TEAM WILL BE ORGANIZED,
VALUABLE EXISTING DATASETS WILL BE ASSEMBLED, AND PRELIMINARY ANALYSES WILL BE UNDERTAKEN. THEN, WE WILL PURSUE TARGETED
PROJECTS IN YEARS 2 THROUGH 5 OF THE PROJECT THAT ARE EXPECTED TO DEMONSTRATE THE VALUE OF DAS FOR FULL-SCALE DEPLOYMENTS
AND EXPLORE POSSIBLE EXTENSIONS TO THE CURRENT STATE-OF-THE-ART, PARTICULARLY IN THE CONTEXT OF EXPLOSION DETECTION AND
MONITORING."

subaward_description:
POLYMERIC BASED PT MATERIALS

APPROACH (1): SELF-ASSEMBLY OF BLOCK COPOLYMERS AND NPS; YEARS 1-4

THE THOMAS TEAM WILL WORK WITH BINARY MIXTURES OF HIGH-MOLECULAR WEIGHT BLOCK COPOLYMERS (BCPS) AND NANOPARTICLES (NPS) TO FABRICATE HIERARCHICAL 1D, 2D, AND 3D PATTERNS THAT EXHIBIT PERIODIC GAIN (G) AND LOSS (L) REGIONS. CONTROL OF THE NP CORE SIZE AND SURFACE CHEMISTRY OF THE NPS AS WELL AS MOLECULAR WEIGHT AND VOLUME FRACTION OF THE BCPS ALLOWS LOCALIZATION OF NANOPARTICLES TO SPECIFIC REGIONS WITHIN THE SELF-ASSEMBLED BCP. WE WILL STUDY THE CONTROL OF SPATIAL DISTRIBUTION OF VARIOUS SEMICONDUCTOR AND INORGANIC NPS WHICH ACT AS GAIN AND LOSS MEDIA. THE INHERENT LATTICE CONSTANT OF SELF-ASSEMBLED BCPS CAN BE TUNED BY EXPANSION USING SOLVENT SWELLING AS PREVIOUSLY DEMONSTRATED IN BCP PHOTONIC GELS WHERE THE ORIGINAL LATTICE CONSTANT WAS EXPANDED MORE THAN 500%.   IN ADDITION TO DIBLOCKS, TERBLOCK POLYMERS WHEREIN BLOCKS A AND C POSSESS THE SAME INDEX OF REFRACTION, BUT ARE SEQUESTER DIFFERENT CÃ‚Â­ FUNCTIONALIZED INORGANIC (LOSS/RED) AND A-FUNCTIONALIZED QUANTUM DOT (GAIN/GREEN) NANOPARTICLES, LEADING TO A PT-SYMMETRIC PLANE IN THE CENTER OF THE B BLOCK. WHILE BOTH OF THESE EXAMPLES ARE OF 10 SYSTEMS (HIGHLY APPLICABLE TO PT SYMMETRY APPLICATIONS), BLOCK COPOLYMERS CAN ALSO ASSEMBLE INTO A WIDE VARIETY OF 2D 3D PERIODIC ARCHITECTURES WHICH COULD ENABLE A RICH VARIETY OF DYNAMIC BEHAVIORS BY INSERTION OF PT-SYMMETRIC GAIN AND LOSS.

APPROACH (2): OPTICAL LITHOGRAPHY AND PHOTOBLEACHING; YEARS 1-3

INTERFERENCE LITHOGRAPHY (IL) AND 3D DIRECT WRITE (3DDW) LITHOGRAPHY ARE POWERFUL TOOLS TO APPROACH THE FABRICATION OF PT-SYMMETRIC MATERIALS. IL USES A PERIODIC OR QUASI-PERIODIC INTENSITY FIELD BY THE INTERFERENCE OF TWO OR MORE MUTUALLY COHERENT LIGHT BEAMS OF DIFFERENT DIRECTIONS, POLARIZATIONS, PHASES AND AMPLITUDES, TO PATTERN PHOTOCHEMICAL POLYMERS OR PHOTORESISTS. THE THOMAS GROUP HAS EXTENSIVELY USED IL TO CREATE PERIODIC WIDE-AREA AND WELL-ORDERED 2D/3D MICROSTRUCTURES. TO FABRICATE PERIODICALLY DISTRIBUTED GAIN/LOSS REGIONS IN 2D/3D, WE WILL INVESTIGATE BACKFILLING, PHOTO-BLEACHING, AND DYNAMIC GAIN. IN THE FIRST APPROACH, AS PHOTO-CROSS-LINKED POLYMERS CAN BE FURTHER MODIFIED BY THE POST HEAT TREATMENT TO HAVE AN OPTICAL LOSS, A BICONTINUOUS POLYMER/AIR TEMPLATE FABRICATED BY IL CAN BE CONVERTED TO A LOSSY TEMPLATE MATERIAL AND THEN A 30 BICONTINUOUS PT SYMMETRIC MATERIAL CAN BE FORMED BY BACKFILLING OF THE INTERSTITIAL AIR VOLUME WITH DIBLOCK COPOLYMER IN WHICH ONLY ONE DOMAIN CONTAINS ORGANIC DYE MOLECULES OR NPS AND THE OTHER DOMAIN PREFERENTIALLY WETS THE SURFACE OF THE LOSSY TEMPLATE. IN THE PHOTO-BLEACHING APPROACH, WE WILL USE CERTAIN ORGANIC DYE MOLECULES THAT CAN BE PHOTO-BLEACHED BY EITHER DIRECT OR PHOTOCHEMICAL MEANS TO CREATE A PERIODIC GAIN-LOSS MATERIAL. 2D/3D PERIODIC STRUCTURES WITH UNIFORM DOPING CAN BE PERIODICALLY BLEACHED BY IL OR 3DDW, THUS THE INITIAL HOMOGENEOUS GAIN PROFILE BECOMES A SPATIALLY VARYING GAIN AND LOSS PROFILE. ANOTHER POSSIBILITY IS TO USE THE IL EXPOSURE AS THE PUMP OF A GAIN DYE TO INTRODUCE AN INSTANTANEOUS PERIODIC GAIN DISTRIBUTION. IN THIS WAY, PT-SYMMETRY CAN BE INTRODUCED IN A DYNAMIC AND TUNABLE FASHION, BY TURNING ON AND OFF THE EXCITATION OR VARYING THE BEAM PARAMETERS TO ADJUST THE SPATIAL GAIN DISTRIBUTION. THIS COULD LEAD TO, FOR EXAMPLE, TEMPORARILY INDUCED PT STRUCTURES THAT CAN POSSESS MULTIPLE FUNCTIONALITIES, FOR EXAMPLE, A MICROSCALE OPTICAL FILTER WITH TUNABLE-WAVELENGTH, NARROW-BAND RESPONSE.

"DISTRIBUTED ACOUSTIC SENSING (DAS) IS A RAPIDLY EVOLVING TECHNOLOGY THAT USES FIBER-OPTIC CABLES AS ARRAYS OF SENSORS CAPABLE
OF MEASURING SPATIALLY RESOLVED DYNAMIC STRAIN. DAS HAS THE POTENTIAL TO REVOLUTIONIZE SEISMIC MONITORING BY DRAMATICALLY
INCREASING MEASUREMENT DENSITY OVER LARGE DISTANCES THAT CAN REACH TENS OF KILOMETERS, AND BY MAKING OBSERVATIONS IN
DIVERSE ENVIRONMENTS SUCH AS IN CITIES OR ON THE OCEAN FLOOR, IN WHICH TRADITIONAL SEISMIC MONITORING WOULD BE EITHER
DIFFICULT OR IMPOSSIBLE. DAS DIFFERS FROM CONVENTIONAL SEISMIC MONITORING BECAUSE THE FUNDAMENTAL MEASUREMENT BEARS A
MORE COMPLEX RELATIONSHIP TO THE CAUSATIVE WAVEFIELD. FOR THAT REASON, IT IS ESSENTIAL TO UNDERSTAND HOW IT IS SIMILAR TO,
AND HOW IT DIFFERS FROM, CONVENTIONAL SEISMIC MEASUREMENTS. THE GOAL OF THE PROPOSED PROJECT IS TO DEVELOP A QUANTITATIVE
UNDERSTANDING OF THE POTENTIAL OF DAS FOR SEISMIC MONITORING USING DATA COLLECTED IN A WIDE RANGE OF ENVIRONMENTS.
THE PROPOSED PROGRAM OF RESEARCH WILL SUPPORT A BROAD EFFORT OF LEADING DAS RESEARCHERS AT MULTIPLE INSTITUTIONS WITH THE
AIM OF CARRYING OUT RESEARCH THAT WILL LEAD TO A COMPREHENSIVE UNDERSTANDING OF THE STRENGTHS AND LIMITATIONS OF THIS NEW
TECHNOLOGY SUCH THAT ITS POTENTIAL FOR SEISMIC MONITORING CAN BE FULLY REALIZED. IN YEAR 1, THE PROJECT TEAM WILL BE ORGANIZED,
VALUABLE EXISTING DATASETS WILL BE ASSEMBLED, AND PRELIMINARY ANALYSES WILL BE UNDERTAKEN. THEN, WE WILL PURSUE TARGETED
PROJECTS IN YEARS 2 THROUGH 5 OF THE PROJECT THAT ARE EXPECTED TO DEMONSTRATE THE VALUE OF DAS FOR FULL-SCALE DEPLOYMENTS
AND EXPLORE POSSIBLE EXTENSIONS TO THE CURRENT STATE-OF-THE-ART, PARTICULARLY IN THE CONTEXT OF EXPLOSION DETECTION AND
MONITORING."

subaward_description: THE SUBAWARDEE WILL DEVELOP ADAPTIVE SENSING STRATEGIES BASED ON DISTILLED COMPRESSIVE SENSING WITH AIM OF MAINTAINING SENSING PERFORMANCE WHILE MINIMIZING MEASUREMENTS ACQUIRED. prime_award_base_transaction_description: Ultra-stable, phase sensitive, snapshot OCT system enabled by 2-Photon additive manufacturing - In this proposal we aim to build a platform technology for volumetric OCT snapshot imaging using principle of Image Mapping Spectroscopy. We will demonstrate Full Field Spectral Domain OCT ((FFSDOCT)) system in free space. The volumetric functional OCT will be enabled by leveraging advanced 2Photon Polymerization 3-D printing approaches, that will permit printing of arbitrary optical quality structures. To achieve these goals, we will utilize state of the art Quantum X system from Nanoscribe. The device allows the combination of small, and medium size detail allowing feature sizes down to 140 nm. The roughness obtained in the printing process is below 20 nm. Prints will be performed in clear resins (IV-Dip, SU8 analogs etc.). It also permits unprecedented print volumes in comparison to other 2PP printers. Specifically, the proof of concept Full Field Spectral Domain OCT will use custom designed 3-D printed multifaceted mirror imaged and dispersed onto a 2-D sCMOS sensor. The mapping mirror will incorporate 10,000 miniature facets and 100 unique tilts to map 100x100 image points onto a camera sensor. In result the system will operate as an array of parallel, high resolution spectrometers where the number of spectrometers equals the number of object points. The resulting FFSDOCT system will have no moving parts yet be capable of acquiring volumetric OCT images at the frame rate of the sensor (30 Hz). The imaging spectrometer will linearly sample in wavenumber. To evaluate system, we will perform series of imaging experiments in free space. FFSDOCT will be characterized for resolution, system sensitivity, the measurement of flow and nanoscale vibrations.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: OVERALL GOAL: WE PROPOSE TO INVESTIGATE THE RELATIVE ROLES OF COULOMB COLLISIONS  LOCALLY ENHANCED CONVECTION ELECTRIC FIELDS  AND PLASMA WAVES DAMPING  IN TRANSFERRING ENERGY FROM THE RING CURRENT TO THE IONOSPHERE-THERMOSPHERE  USING A COMBINATION OF IN SITU SATELLITE PARTICLE AND FIELD DATA IN THE INNER MAGNETOSPHERE AND SUBAURORAL RED-LINE SAR ARC EMISSIONS AS A DIAGNOSTIC OF THESE PROCESSES. MOTIVATION: OBSERVATIONS OF SAR ARCS (SPECTRALLY PURE 630.0 NM EMISSION) BY ALL-SKY IMAGERS (ASIS) SUGGEST THAT THEY ARE MORE COMPLICATED THAN PREVIOUSLY THOUGHT  WITH INCONSISTENCIES BETWEEN OBSERVATIONS AND PUBLISHED THEORY. THE OCCURRENCE OF LOCALIZED  ENHANCED  POLEWARD-DIRECTED  SUB-AURORAL ELECTRIC FIELDS (TERMED SAPS OR SAID) PRESENT A POSSIBLE MECHANISM FOR THE PRODUCTION OF SAR ARC EMISSIONS PREVIOUSLY NOT CONSIDERED DUE TO LACK OF OBSERVATIONS AND UNDERSTANDING. WE PROPOSE TO CONDUCT A SYSTEMATIC STUDY OF INNER MAGNETOSPHERIC PARTICLE AND FIELD OBSERVATIONS USING DATA FROM THE VAN ALLEN PROBES (VAP) AND THEMIS SPACECRAFT  ALONG WITH SUB-AURORAL IONOSPHERIC DATA OF SAR ARCS FROM ASIS  AND CO-LOCATED F-REGION DATA FROM DMSP SPACECRAFT AND MILLSTONE HILL INCOHERENT SCATTER RADAR. SCIENCE QUESTIONS: WE WILL LOOK AT LOSS OF RING CURRENT ENERGY VIA COULOMB COLLISIONS OF RING CURRENT IONS WITH COLD PLASMASPHERIC ELECTRONS  AND STUDY THE EXTENT TO WHICH SAPS ELECTRIC FIELDS PLAY A ROLE IN PRODUCING SAR ARC EMISSIONS. THE SPECIFIC SCIENCE QUESTIONS ARE: (A) HOW ARE SAR ARCS RELATED TO THE RING CURRENT AND PLASMASPHERE? (B) WHAT ROLE DO COULOMB COLLISIONS PLAY IN PROVIDING ENERGY TO POWER SAR ARCS? (C) WHAT IS THE ROLE OF LARGE SUB-AURORAL ELECTRIC FIELDS IN SAR ARC GENERATION? (D) ARE THERE CONSISTENT SIGNATURES OF EMIC (OR OTHER) WAVES IN REGIONS THAT MAP TO OBSERVED SAR ARCS? METHODOLOGY: IT CONSISTS OF DATA ANALYSIS OF VAP AND THEMIS DATA FOR CASES OF OBSERVED SAR ARCS IDENTIFIED IN ASI DATA  SUPPLEMENTED BY RADAR F-REGION AND DMSP TOPSIDE DATA  AND BY NUMERICAL SIMULATIONS. WE WILL CLASSIFY THE OBSERVED SAR ARCS IN TERMS OF GEOMAGNETIC CONDITIONS AND STORM PHASE. DATA FROM EIGHT ASIS AT SUB-AURORAL LATITUDES (L~ 3) WITH BROAD LONGITUDINAL DISTRIBUTION WILL BE ANALYZED. WE WILL THEN SEARCH THE COMBINED DATA FROM VAP HOPE  RBSPICE  EMFISIS  AND EFW INSTRUMENTS. WE WILL CHARACTERIZE DC ELECTRIC FIELDS  RING CURRENT PARTICLE FLUXES AND ANISOTROPY  AND LOCATION OF THE PLASMAPAUSE. ON THEMIS  ION AND ELECTRON PARTICLE DATA FROM ESA AND SST INSTRUMENTS AND DENSITY DATA FROM THE SPACECRAFT POTENTIAL WILL BE USED. MILLSTONE HILL RADAR DATA (WHEN AVAILABLE) WILL PROVIDE F-REGION PLASMA DENSITY AND TEMPERATURE HEIGHT PROFILES. WE WILL USE EVENT SIMULATIONS WITH THE SAMI3-RCM FIRST-PRINCIPLES MODEL OF THE RING CURRENT AND IONOSPHERE. CONSTRAINED BY DATA  SIMULATIONS WILL BE USED TO COMPUTE SIGNATURES OF RING CURRENT ENERGY LOSSES AND IONOSPHERIC ELECTRON HEATING RATES. OUR COMPARISON WITH RESULTS OF DATA ANALYSIS WILL ALLOWS US TO VALIDATE THEORETICAL PREDICTIONS OR FIND SYSTEMATIC DISCREPANCIES. WORK PLAN: THE PROPOSED WORK INCLUDES: 1) IDENTIFICATION OF STORM PERIODS AND AVAILABLE IN SITU AND GROUND-BASED DATA; 2) COMPUTATION OF FOOTPRINTS OF VAP AND THEMIS TO IDENTIFY OVERFLIGHTS IN BOTH HEMISPHERES THAT MAP INTO THE IONOSPHERE NEAR L=3; 3) ANALYSIS OF THE DATA; 4) NUMERICAL SIMULATIONS TO OBTAIN KEY OUTPUTS; AND 5) CALCULATION OF BRIGHTNESS LEVELS OF RED-LINE EMISSIONS IN REGIONS OF SAR ARCS DUE TO COULOMB COLLISIONS AND SAPS ELECTRIC FIELDS. RELEVANCE: THE PROPOSED WORK IS RELEVANT TO ONE OF THE FOUR SCIENCE GOALS FROM THE DECADAL SURVEY AS IT ADDRESS THE DYNAMICS AND COUPLING OF EARTH S MAGNETOSPHERE  IONOSPHERE  AND ATMOSPHERE. IT ALSO ADDRESSES ONE OF THE SCIENCE OBJECTIVES OF VAP BY CONTRIBUTING TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF HOW AND WHY THE RING CURRENT VARIES DURING STORMS.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: Ultra-stable, phase sensitive, snapshot OCT system enabled by 2-Photon additive manufacturing - In this proposal we aim to build a platform technology for volumetric OCT snapshot imaging using principle of Image Mapping Spectroscopy. We will demonstrate Full Field Spectral Domain OCT ((FFSDOCT)) system in free space. The volumetric functional OCT will be enabled by leveraging advanced 2Photon Polymerization 3-D printing approaches, that will permit printing of arbitrary optical quality structures. To achieve these goals, we will utilize state of the art Quantum X system from Nanoscribe. The device allows the combination of small, and medium size detail allowing feature sizes down to 140 nm. The roughness obtained in the printing process is below 20 nm. Prints will be performed in clear resins (IV-Dip, SU8 analogs etc.). It also permits unprecedented print volumes in comparison to other 2PP printers. Specifically, the proof of concept Full Field Spectral Domain OCT will use custom designed 3-D printed multifaceted mirror imaged and dispersed onto a 2-D sCMOS sensor. The mapping mirror will incorporate 10,000 miniature facets and 100 unique tilts to map 100x100 image points onto a camera sensor. In result the system will operate as an array of parallel, high resolution spectrometers where the number of spectrometers equals the number of object points. The resulting FFSDOCT system will have no moving parts yet be capable of acquiring volumetric OCT images at the frame rate of the sensor (30 Hz). The imaging spectrometer will linearly sample in wavenumber. To evaluate system, we will perform series of imaging experiments in free space. FFSDOCT will be characterized for resolution, system sensitivity, the measurement of flow and nanoscale vibrations.

prime_awardee_business_types: Nonprofit with 501C3 IRS Status (Other than an Institution of Higher Education)
prime_award_base_transaction_description: OVERALL GOAL: WE PROPOSE TO INVESTIGATE THE RELATIVE ROLES OF COULOMB COLLISIONS  LOCALLY ENHANCED CONVECTION ELECTRIC FIELDS  AND PLASMA WAVES DAMPING  IN TRANSFERRING ENERGY FROM THE RING CURRENT TO THE IONOSPHERE-THERMOSPHERE  USING A COMBINATION OF IN SITU SATELLITE PARTICLE AND FIELD DATA IN THE INNER MAGNETOSPHERE AND SUBAURORAL RED-LINE SAR ARC EMISSIONS AS A DIAGNOSTIC OF THESE PROCESSES. MOTIVATION: OBSERVATIONS OF SAR ARCS (SPECTRALLY PURE 630.0 NM EMISSION) BY ALL-SKY IMAGERS (ASIS) SUGGEST THAT THEY ARE MORE COMPLICATED THAN PREVIOUSLY THOUGHT  WITH INCONSISTENCIES BETWEEN OBSERVATIONS AND PUBLISHED THEORY. THE OCCURRENCE OF LOCALIZED  ENHANCED  POLEWARD-DIRECTED  SUB-AURORAL ELECTRIC FIELDS (TERMED SAPS OR SAID) PRESENT A POSSIBLE MECHANISM FOR THE PRODUCTION OF SAR ARC EMISSIONS PREVIOUSLY NOT CONSIDERED DUE TO LACK OF OBSERVATIONS AND UNDERSTANDING. WE PROPOSE TO CONDUCT A SYSTEMATIC STUDY OF INNER MAGNETOSPHERIC PARTICLE AND FIELD OBSERVATIONS USING DATA FROM THE VAN ALLEN PROBES (VAP) AND THEMIS SPACECRAFT  ALONG WITH SUB-AURORAL IONOSPHERIC DATA OF SAR ARCS FROM ASIS  AND CO-LOCATED F-REGION DATA FROM DMSP SPACECRAFT AND MILLSTONE HILL INCOHERENT SCATTER RADAR. SCIENCE QUESTIONS: WE WILL LOOK AT LOSS OF RING CURRENT ENERGY VIA COULOMB COLLISIONS OF RING CURRENT IONS WITH COLD PLASMASPHERIC ELECTRONS  AND STUDY THE EXTENT TO WHICH SAPS ELECTRIC FIELDS PLAY A ROLE IN PRODUCING SAR ARC EMISSIONS. THE SPECIFIC SCIENCE QUESTIONS ARE: (A) HOW ARE SAR ARCS RELATED TO THE RING CURRENT AND PLASMASPHERE? (B) WHAT ROLE DO COULOMB COLLISIONS PLAY IN PROVIDING ENERGY TO POWER SAR ARCS? (C) WHAT IS THE ROLE OF LARGE SUB-AURORAL ELECTRIC FIELDS IN SAR ARC GENERATION? (D) ARE THERE CONSISTENT SIGNATURES OF EMIC (OR OTHER) WAVES IN REGIONS THAT MAP TO OBSERVED SAR ARCS? METHODOLOGY: IT CONSISTS OF DATA ANALYSIS OF VAP AND THEMIS DATA FOR CASES OF OBSERVED SAR ARCS IDENTIFIED IN ASI DATA  SUPPLEMENTED BY RADAR F-REGION AND DMSP TOPSIDE DATA  AND BY NUMERICAL SIMULATIONS. WE WILL CLASSIFY THE OBSERVED SAR ARCS IN TERMS OF GEOMAGNETIC CONDITIONS AND STORM PHASE. DATA FROM EIGHT ASIS AT SUB-AURORAL LATITUDES (L~ 3) WITH BROAD LONGITUDINAL DISTRIBUTION WILL BE ANALYZED. WE WILL THEN SEARCH THE COMBINED DATA FROM VAP HOPE  RBSPICE  EMFISIS  AND EFW INSTRUMENTS. WE WILL CHARACTERIZE DC ELECTRIC FIELDS  RING CURRENT PARTICLE FLUXES AND ANISOTROPY  AND LOCATION OF THE PLASMAPAUSE. ON THEMIS  ION AND ELECTRON PARTICLE DATA FROM ESA AND SST INSTRUMENTS AND DENSITY DATA FROM THE SPACECRAFT POTENTIAL WILL BE USED. MILLSTONE HILL RADAR DATA (WHEN AVAILABLE) WILL PROVIDE F-REGION PLASMA DENSITY AND TEMPERATURE HEIGHT PROFILES. WE WILL USE EVENT SIMULATIONS WITH THE SAMI3-RCM FIRST-PRINCIPLES MODEL OF THE RING CURRENT AND IONOSPHERE. CONSTRAINED BY DATA  SIMULATIONS WILL BE USED TO COMPUTE SIGNATURES OF RING CURRENT ENERGY LOSSES AND IONOSPHERIC ELECTRON HEATING RATES. OUR COMPARISON WITH RESULTS OF DATA ANALYSIS WILL ALLOWS US TO VALIDATE THEORETICAL PREDICTIONS OR FIND SYSTEMATIC DISCREPANCIES. WORK PLAN: THE PROPOSED WORK INCLUDES: 1) IDENTIFICATION OF STORM PERIODS AND AVAILABLE IN SITU AND GROUND-BASED DATA; 2) COMPUTATION OF FOOTPRINTS OF VAP AND THEMIS TO IDENTIFY OVERFLIGHTS IN BOTH HEMISPHERES THAT MAP INTO THE IONOSPHERE NEAR L=3; 3) ANALYSIS OF THE DATA; 4) NUMERICAL SIMULATIONS TO OBTAIN KEY OUTPUTS; AND 5) CALCULATION OF BRIGHTNESS LEVELS OF RED-LINE EMISSIONS IN REGIONS OF SAR ARCS DUE TO COULOMB COLLISIONS AND SAPS ELECTRIC FIELDS. RELEVANCE: THE PROPOSED WORK IS RELEVANT TO ONE OF THE FOUR SCIENCE GOALS FROM THE DECADAL SURVEY AS IT ADDRESS THE DYNAMICS AND COUPLING OF EARTH S MAGNETOSPHERE  IONOSPHERE  AND ATMOSPHERE. IT ALSO ADDRESSES ONE OF THE SCIENCE OBJECTIVES OF VAP BY CONTRIBUTING TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF HOW AND WHY THE RING CURRENT VARIES DURING STORMS.

subaward_description: THE PROPOSED WORK AT RICE UNIVERSITY INCLUDES A MODELING COMPONENT USING THE RICE CONVECTION MODEL DEVELOPED BY THE MAGNETOSPHERIC PHYSICS GROUP AT RICE UNIVERSITY. STANISLAV SAZYKIN WILL SET UP AND RUN NUMERICAL SIMULATIONS AS DESCRIBED IN THE TEXT OF THE PROPOSAL, AND HELP ANALYZE AND INTERPRET SIMULATION RESULTS AND MODEL-DATA COMPARISON, AS WELL AS PREPARE PUBLICATIONS OF THE MODEL RESULTS.

subaward_description: RICE UNIVERSITY ROLE IN THE NSF 1-CORPS NODE: RICE UNIVERSITY WILL PARTNER AND COLLABORATE WITH THE UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN AND TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY IN ORDER TO PROMOTE AND EXECUTE THE GOALS AND OBJECTIVES OF THE NSF 1-CORPS NODE IN THE STATE O F TEXAS, THE SOUTHWEST ALLIANCE FOR ENTREPRENEURIAL INNOVATION NODE (SAEIN) RICE UNIVERSITY WILL HELP DEVELOP AND PROVIDE INPUT INTO THE STRATEGIC GOALS AND PLANS FOR THE NODE. RICE UNIVERSITY WILL HELP ORGANIZE AND HOST NSF 1-CORPS NATIONAL COHORTS AND REGIONAL 1-CORPS EVENTS. RICE UNIVERSITY WILL PROVIDE QUALIFIED INSTRUCTORS TO BECOME CERTIFIED TO TEACH THE NATIONALAND REGIONAL NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM. RICE WILL HOST A NATIONAL COHORT OF THE NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM IN HOUSTON EACH YEAR, IF DESIRED. RICE WILL MARKET THE NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM IN ORDER TO ENCOURAGE TEAMS TO APPLY FOR NSF 1-CORPS GRANTS, NOT ONLY TO RICE UNIVERSITY FACULTY AND STAFF, BUT ALSO TO THE UNIVERSITY OF HOUSTON. RICE WILL ORGANIZE AND PUBLICIZE NSF 1- CORPS INFORMATION SESS IONS TO ENCOURAGE FACULTY AND STUDENT PARTICIPATION. RICE UNIVERSITY WILL ASSIST IN MARKETING THE NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM TO OTHERUNIVERSITIES, AS APPROPRIATE, AND ASSIST IN MARKETING THE PROGRAM TO UNIVERSITIES IN ADJACENT STATES, WHEN THE NODE IS READ Y TO EXPAND ITS REACH. RICE WILL ASSIST WITH HOSTING AND/OR ATTENDING QUARTERLY MEETINGS OF THE NSF 1-CORPS NODE.

subaward_description:
RICE UNIVERSITY ROLE IN THE NSF 1-CORPS NODE:
RICE UNIVERSITY WILL PARTNER AND COLLABORATE WITH THE UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS AT AUSTIN AND TEXAS A&M UNIVERSITY IN
ORDER TO PROMOTE AND EXECUTE THE GOALS AND OBJECTIVES OF THE NSF 1-CORPS NODE IN THE STATE OF TEXAS, THE
SOUTHWEST ALLIANCE FOR ENTREPRENEURIAL INNOVATION NODE (SAEIN)
RICE UNIVERSITY WILL HELP DEVELOP AND PROVIDE INPUT INTO THE STRATEGIC GOALS AND PLANS FOR THE NODE. RICE UNIVERSITY
WILL HELP ORGANIZE AND HOST NSF 1-CORPS NATIONAL COHORTS AND REGIONAL 1-CORPS EVENTS. RICE UNIVERSITY WILL
PROVIDE QUALIFIED INSTRUCTORS TO BECOME CERTIFIED TO TEACH THE NATIONAL AND REGIONAL NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM. RICE WILL
HOST A NATIONAL COHORT OF THE NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM IN HOUSTON EACH YEAR, IF DESIRED.
RICE WILL MARKET THE NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM IN ORDER TO ENCOURAGE TEAMS TO APPLY FOR NSF 1-CORPS GRANTS, NOT ONLY TO
RICE UNIVERSITY FACULTY AND STAFF, BUT ALSO TO THE UNIVERSITY OF HOUSTON. RICE WILL ORGANIZE AND PUBLICIZE NSF 1-
CORPS INFORMATION SESSIONS TO ENCOURAGE FACULTY AND STUDENT PARTICIPATION. RICE UNIVERSITY WILL ASSIST IN MARKETING
THE NSF 1-CORPS PROGRAM TO OTHER UNIVERSITIES, AS APPROPRIATE, AND ASSIST IN MARKETING THE PROGRAM TO UNIVERSITIES
IN ADJACENT STATES, WHEN THE NODE IS READY TO EXPAND ITS REACH.
RICE WILL ASSIST WITH HOSTING AND/OR ATTENDING QUARTERLY MEETINGS OF THE NSF 1-CORPS NODE.

subawardee_business_types: PRIVATE UNIVERSITY OR COLLEGE,OTHER NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION
subaward_description:
AGREEMENT: $50,000
AMEND 1: $50,000

CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR (CAR) T CELLS HAVE REVOLUTIONIZED THE TREATMENT OF CERTAIN HEMATOLOGIC MALIGNANCIES AND INFECTIOUS DISEASES. REFRACTORY CD19 POSITIVE B CELL LYMPHOMAS AND LYMPHOBLASTIC LEUKEMIA IN PARTICULAR HAVE SHOWN HIGH RESPONSE RATES TO AUTOLOGOUS DELIVERED THERAPEUTIC CAR T CELLS. DESPITE THIS EXCITING PROGRESS, MOST PATIENTS WHO RECEIVE THESE THERAPIES FAIL TO DISPLAY LONG TERM DISEASE CONTROL, AND FURTHERMORE, ADVERSE EVENTS; SUCH AS CYTOKINE RELEASE SYNDROME (CRS) AND/OR NEUROLOGICAL TOXICITY, HAVE ALSO BEEN OBSERVED IN PATIENTS TREATED WITH CAR T CELLS. MOREOVER, CAR T CELLS THAT CAN ROBUSTLY TARGET AND ELIMINATE SOLID TUMORS HAVE YET TO DESIGNED. THE THERAPEUTIC OBSTACLE PREVENTING CAR T EFFECTIVENESS IN SOLID TUMORS IS THOUGHT TO LARGELY STEM FROM SEVERAL HOSTILE FEATURES ASSOCIATED WITH THE SOLID TUMOR MICROENVIRONMENT (TME), MOST IMPORTANTLY FOR THIS PROPOSAL ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â‚¬Å“ THE IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE AND IMMUNOLOGICALLY EXHAUSTIVE SIGNALING EVENTS THAT PREVENT OPTIMAL CAR T CELL EFFICACY AND PERSISTENCE. IMPORTANTLY, IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE AND IMMUNOLOGICALLY EXHAUSTIVE SIGNALING ARE ALSO CRITICAL FACTORS THAT INFLUENCE THE EFFECTIVENESS OF CAR T CELLS IN THE TREATMENT OF HEMATOLOGIC MALIGNANCIES, INCLUDING LYMPHOMAS AND CHRONIC LYMPHOCYTIC LEUKEMIA. THEREFORE, COLLECTIVELY AN INCREASED UNDERSTANDING OF HOW T CELLS AND CAR T CELLS SUCCUMB TO EXHAUSTION AND PROCESS IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE SIGNALS, AND THE DEVELOPMENT OF NEW TECHNOLOGIES TO PREVENT, RESHAPE, OR BALANCE THESE PATHWAYS TO PREVENT CYTOTOXICITY AND/OR IMPROVE FUNCTION, HAS THE POTENTIAL TO SHIFT THE THERAPEUTIC LANDSCAPE FOR PATIENTS SUFFERING FROM LYMPHOMA, CHRONIC LYMPHOCYTIC LEUKEMIA, AND IN THE LONGER-TERM CANCER IN GENERAL.

subaward_description: THIS PROPOSAL SEEKS TO ESTABLISH A "CENTER FOR RISK BASED COMMUNITY RESILIENCE PLANNING" AND SPECIFICALLY DEVELOP A COMPUTATIONAL MODELING ENVIRONMENT FOR COMMUNITY RESILIENCE, ALONG WITH DATA MANAGEMENT TOOLS AND RESILIENCE DATA ARCHITECTURE VALIDATION EXERCISES. PROFESSOR PADGETT WILL SERVE AS THE PI AT RICE UNIVERSITY (WITH LEAD PI AT COLORADO STATE UNIVERSITY, JOHN VAN DE LINDT). HER ROLE IN THE CENTER WILL BE AS A RESEARCHER AND A NIST LIAISON WITHIN THE ENGINEERING TEAM. A COMPUTATIONAL PLATFORM WILL BE DEVELOPED FOR RISK ASSESSMENT A RESILIENCY QUANTIFICATION OF BUILDING AND INTERDEPENDENT INFRASTRUCTURE SUBJECTED TO MULTIPLE HAZARDS, SUCH AS EARTHQUAKES, HURRICANES, AND TORNADOS. PROFESSOR PADGETT WILL SUPPORT THE DEVELOPMENT OF PARAMETERIZED FRAGILITY MODELS FOR INFRASTRUCTURE COMPONENTS AND THEIR NETWORKS, SUCH AS BRIDGE AND TRANSPORTATION INFRASTRUCTURE, UNDER MULTIPLE HAZARDS INCLUDING AGING AND DETERIORATION; THE FORMULATION OF SINGLE- AND MULTI-HAZARD RISK ASSESSMENTS AND RESILIENCY PERFORMANCE METRICS; AND DERIVATION OF INDIVIDUAL PERFORMANCE TARGETS REQUIRED TO ACHIEVE COMMUNITY RESILIENCE TARGETS. FURTHERMORE, VALIDATION STUDIES WILL BE CONDUCTED AT MULTIPLE SCALES USING PAST EVENTS SUCH AS HURRICANES KATRINA OR IKE. PROFESSOR PADGETT WILL COLLABORATE WITH OTHER CENTER TEAM MEMBERS, INCLUDING COORDINATION WITH RESEARCHERS AT CSU AND UIUC AS WELL AS NIST. RELATED ACTIVITIES INCLUDE PARTICIPATING IN COORDINATION MEETINGS AND CONDUCTING WORKSHOPS TO SEEK FEEDBACK ON THE COMPUTATIONAL MODELING ENVIRONMENT AND OTHER RESEARCH PRODUCTS.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: Evaluating the impact of human milk oligosaccharides on the vaginal microenvironment - Project Summary Formation and maturation of the human microbiota is associated with lifelong health outcomes, and its initial composition is heavily influenced by the maternal vaginal microbiota. Perturbations to the maternal vaginal microbiota in pregnancy or intrapartum are associated with increased risk of multiple health disorders from preterm birth to developing immune or metabolic disease later in life. Factors that promote an optimal transfer of microbes to the infant are undefined, and to date, knowledge of this critical process is largely descriptive or correlative. We seek to overcome this barrier by applying recent advances in model development, particularly the use of gnotobiotic animals, three-dimensional tissue culture, and cultivation of complex microbial communities, to generate experimental evidence for host and microbial factors shaping the maternal vaginal environment during pregnancy. With these models in hand, we will test a hypothesis proposing an entirely novel mechanism of maternal influence on the initial infant microbiota. Breastfeeding is consistently associated with improved infant health, reduced risk of infectious disease, and accelerated immune and microbial maturation within the gut. Human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs), the third most abundant component of breastmilk, serve as prebiotics for the developing microbiota and shape immune development and tolerance at the gut epithelium. Quite recently, it has become apparent that HMOs are systemically present during pregnancy, suggesting a wider influence on maternal and fetal biology than previously appreciated. Clinical studies have correlated maternal serum HMOs with vaginal microbiome signatures and genetic variants in HMO production are linked with vaginal pathogen colonization and risk for preterm birth. The central hypothesis of this proposal is that HMOs modulate the maternal vaginal microenvironment in pregnancy to promote growth of beneficial microbes for seeding the infant gut and to concurrently support barrier function and limit maternal inflammation. We will test this hypothesis by: 1) Characterizing the biological activity of HMOs towards human vaginal communities in vitro and in humanized gnotobiotic mice, and 2) Evaluating immunomodulatory impacts of HMOs at the vaginal mucosa using human vaginal organoids and gnotobiotic and germ-free mouse models. This research approach is a complementary merger of the applicant’s background in microbial pathogenesis and animal models of vaginal colonization and host-microbiome interactions, and the institutional strengths of BCM in the areas of human microbiome clinical research, human organoid development, and microbiome innovation and therapeutics. This high impact project will apprise the basic biology of HMO activity in the vaginal tract and provide a therapeutic tactic to better harness the potential of HMOs in modulating vaginal microbes and immunity. The breadth of experience necessary to complete this project is readily available through the combination of the applicant’s experience and supporting collaborators, and will lay the foundation for new findings, publications, and an exciting research trajectory.

subaward_description:
AGREEMENT: $66,800
AMD 1: $66,801

AS PART OF THIS PROJECT, RICE UNIVERSITY RESEARCHERS WILL COMPUTATIONALLY IDENTIFY HMO UTILIZATION IN THE GENOMES OF HUMAN VAGINAL BACTERIA. WE WILL SUBSEQUENTLY CHARACTERIZE THE HMO UTILIZATION SYSTEMS TO ELUCIDATE THEIR BIOLOGICAL FUNCTIONS. IN PARTICULAR, WE WILL TREAT THE ORGANISMS WITH HMOS AND USE MRNA AND REPORTER GENE ANALYSIS TO EVALUATE SPECIFIC ACTIVATION OF THESE PATHWAYS. WE WILL ALSO KNOCK THEM OUT AND ASSAY DIFFERENCES IN ORGANISMAL RESPONSES TO THE HMOS. WE MAY ALSO HETEROLOGOUSLY EXPRESS THE PATHWAYS IN MODEL ORGANISMS TO ENABLE MORE RELIABLE CHARACTERIZATION. WE WILL RECEIVE THE GENOMES OF INTEREST AND OTHER DATA (E.G. MICROBIOREACTOR GROWTH DATA) FROM DR. PATRAS AND DR. BRITTON AT BCM, COORDINATE OUR EXPERIMENTS WITH THEM, AND REPORT OUR DATA BACK TO THEM ON A REGULAR (APPROXIMATELY BI-WEEKLY) BASIS VIA EMAIL, VIRTUAL, OR IN-PERSON MEETINGS.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: Cell Based Immunomodulation to Promote Post-Infarct Myocardial Repair - PROJECT SUMMARY Ischemic heart disease is the leading cause of death in the industrialized world. Heart failure (HF) develops in 20-30% of patients after myocardial infarction (MI) due to extensive scarring exacerbated by a persistent 2.9- fold increase of inflammatory macrophages in the infarct borderzone and remote myocardium. Cytokines, such as Interleukin-10 (IL-10), and cytokine inhibitors, such as Interleukin-1 receptor antagonists (IL-1Ra), are potent immune modulators that regulate inflammation, reduce infarct size, and improve ventricular function. However, translation of cytokine therapy to patients has been limited by poor biodistribution, toxicity, and paradoxical pro- inflammatory responses with sustained administration. Local immunomodulation with cell therapy secretion of cytokines holds promise, but poor cell survival (<1%) and reduced in vivo potency have remained critical barriers preventing clinical translation. We have overcome these critical limitations by developing an innovative and translational cell therapy platform that enables sustained locally administration to the heart after MI. We have engineered retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) cells to produce IL-1Ra and IL-10 in a more physiologically relevant paracrine fashion at high local concentrations without systemic absorption and have encapsulated them in alginate-based core-shell capsules, shielding them from the host immune system and allowing them to serve as a regulatable in situ cytokine “factories”. We have demonstrated that local delivery modulates inflammation and improves outcomes after acute MI. In this proposal, we hypothesize that epicardial implantation of RPE-IL10 and RPE-IL1Ra will sustain reductions in post-MI chronic inflammation and reverse cardiomyopathic remodeling in HF. In Aim 1, RPE cell capsule systems will be developed and optimized for sustained 6 month secretion of IL- 10 and IL-1Ra with integration of an apoptotic cell safety switch to cease therapy post-administration. In Aim 2, these IL-10 and IL-1Ra cytokine factories will be validated as mono- or combination therapies in a chronic post- MI rat HF model by confirming reduction of the cardiac inflammasome, promotion of reparative macrophage phenotypes, and reversal of adverse cardiac remodeling. In Aim 3, the ability to titrate and cease therapy post- implantation in a clinically relevant fashion will be evaluated in a porcine model. Efficacy of RPE therapy will be tested in a chronic porcine infarct model over 100 days. The proposed work is expected to result in a new immune modulatory strategy for MI and elucidate mechanisms of cytokine therapy on the cardiac inflammasome. Importantly, the developed biotechnology will be ready for pre-clinical testing and has translational benefit beyond cytokines alone, as it can be utilized for a wide range of protein and local drug delivery directly to the heart.
